text: Feedback keeps us mission-focused. Sharpening requires friction. Without feedback, we drift toward comfort and mediocrity. God has called us to a higher standard. Blind spots hold us back. Others can see what we can't. Feedback gives us the perspective we can't get on our own, helping us to grow. Improvement comes from feedback. If you don't receive feedback, you'll likely repeat the same behavior or continue saying the same thing - there might be a better way. Feedback builds trust. It shows we value each other enough to risk discomfort. Seek honesty, not flattery. Feedback fuels encouragement. Men are strengthened when they hear what they're doing well and how they're making an impact. There are only two non-negotiables: give helpful feedback and cover it in prayer. Feedback is not criticism; it's a gift. It's how iron sharpens iron (Proverbs 27:17).

Ensure ALL Tribesmen know that Feedback is part of our culture and it's how we sharpen each other. Remind them that feedback is coming so no one is caught off guard. Tribe Leaders: set the expectation and build the culture around the fire. Lead by example. Give feedback to your Chiefs and seek feedback from others. Tribe Chiefs: remind Tribesmen that feedback is coming when you reach out to discuss the plan for the week. Provide feedback on the plan and always follow up with feedback after - even for those building the fire.

Use Discernment. There's a time and place. If the Tribesman is going through a major struggle, now is not the time to share a blind spot. Encourage him instead. Generally, feedback is best given privately 1-on-1. Give Feedback Promptly. Feedback is best shared as close to the action as is wise, not weeks later when the details are fuzzy.

Pray First. Ask God to give you courage, humility, discernment, and the right words to say. Make sure your heart is motivated by love, not pride or control. Separate the Do from the Who. The best feedback addresses what we do, not who we are. Focus on specific actions and examples instead of identity. Look Forward. Feedback is forward looking. The focus is not to point out what you did wrong, but how you can improve moving forward. Lead with Encouragement. Begin with something positive. Go Second. Before you provide your own feedback, ask the Tribesman what he thinks went well and what could be improved. Provide Two Strengths and One Opportunity (Feedback Sandwich). Discuss the Impact. Coach for Growth. Utilize the Leadership Guide. Point to the Men's Alliance Leadership Guide for ways to improve. This makes the point objective rather than an opinion.

Seek Feedback. Feedback is essential to growth. If it is not given, seek it out. Trust Your Brother. Believe that your brother's words are meant to sharpen you, not to wound you. Listen Without Defensiveness. Proverbs 12:15 says, the way of a fool is right in his own eyes, but a wise man listens to advice. Being defensive or offended is a choice. Ask Clarifying Questions. Thank the Giver. It takes courage to give feedback. Honor that courage by thanking the person, even if it stings. Reflect and Act. Pray about the feedback and take steps to grow. Don't waste it.

In today's culture the word Christian can mean different things to different people. We can no longer assume that we all have a shared meaning for this word. In fact, if someone asks you, "Are you a Christian?" it might be smart to ask them, "What do you mean by Christian?" before agreeing to their definition - whatever that may be. So, before we dive into this course, let's make sure that we all agree on what exactly it means to be a Christian. A Christian is someone who follows Jesus. It's really that simple. You don't have to go to church to be a Christian. There were Christians before there were churches after all. You don't have to give money in the offering plate to be a Christian. You don't have to be baptized to be a Christian. The thief on the cross beside of Jesus was never baptized. You don't have to read the Bible to be a Christian. In fact, you don't have to be able to read at all. Plus, we had Christians long before we had the Bible. All of those things are great to do, and Christians should certainly strive to do them, but they are not what makes someone a Christian.

In Acts 16:31 a Roman guard asks Paul, "What must I do to be saved?" Paul replied, "Believe in the Lord Jesus Christ and you will be saved." That's it. It's that simple. You might wonder, What exactly does that mean to "believe in." After all, don't even the demons believe that Jesus existed, died, and rose again? Great question! When we say "believe in" what we mean is to put our trust in. To rely on him. Romans 10:9 says, "If you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord, and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved." Saved from what exactly? When you live in a nation where everyone is comfortable and well fed, it might seem like we don't need saving. But God tells us something very different... Romans 3:23 says that every single one of us has sinned. We have all fallen short of God's perfect standard. Romans 6:23 tells us that the payment for our sins (even the smallest ones) is death, separation from God for eternity. This is the hard truth. We are all broken sinners. And a perfect God would not be perfectly just if he ignored those sins or allowed them to go unpunished. But that's where the good news comes in...

Romans 5:8 explains that while we were still sinning, Jesus died for us. He took our punishment and paid our debt for us! That is literally the greatest news on earth! And like the most famous verse in the Bible, John 3:16 says, whoever believes in him will not die, but will have eternal life. That's what it means to be a Christian. It's simply acknowledging our own sinfulness and asking Jesus to credit his death on the cross to our account. Ephesians 2:8-9 explains, "It is by grace you have been saved, through faith, and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God ,  not by works, so that no one can boast." And that is what makes Christianity different from every other religion on the planet. While other religions are all about doing good things, keeping laws, following commandments and pillars, Christianity says you can never be good enough on your own and that's why God came to us to save us. All of our own attempts are futile. We need a savior. And that's why God launched a rescue mission!

Jesus of Nazareth was God in human form. He entered into his own creation and lived with us so that he could take on the sins of the world and die in our place. Then he demonstrated his power over sin and death by walking out of his own grave! In John 10:18, Jesus says, "No one can take my life from me. I sacrifice it voluntarily. For I have the authority to lay it down when I want to and also to take it up again."

Introduction Every Christian should be ready to answer one big question: "Why are you a Christian?" Most of us can tell the story of how we became a Christian ,  a camp experience, a church service, freedom from an addiction, or a moment when we prayed and felt God's presence. Those moments are powerful and personal. But here's the problem: they are your story. Someone from another religion could share their own story with just as much passion. If your only reason for being a Christian is how you felt once, your faith will be shaky when hard times come. What if you lose that feeling? What if you relapse into addiction? What if, like Job or Paul, you lose everything, you're in jail, you feel abandoned, and you're wondering where God is? This is why our foundation must be built on something unshakable - something outside ourselves. The foundation for why we are a Christian must be that it is True. Our why should be based on historical evidence - not personal feelings. How vs. Why - How I became a Christian: the events, experiences, or people that led me to follow Jesus. - Why I am still a Christian today: the truth that keeps me committed, even when life is hard. Your "how" is about your past.

Your "why" is about your foundation. A strong why will keep you going when life is tough. Without it, faith can fall apart like a house of cards. The Danger of a Weak Why Some people believe because: - It makes them feel happy. - They hope it will make life easier or more successful. - They think God will fix all their problems. But Jesus promised something different: "In this world, you will have trouble. But take heart! I have overcome the world." - John 16:33 "If anyone would come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross daily and follow me." - Luke 9:23 Christianity is not a guarantee of comfort. If your why is based on personal improvement, you will walk away when persecution begins. The Only Good Reason The only solid reason to believe anything is because it's true. - If something is true, it deserves your belief. - If it's not true, it doesn't ,  no matter how good it makes you feel. Why am I a Christian? Because Christianity is true. This is not blind faith. Blind faith means believing without evidence ,  and Jesus never asked us to do that. Our Faith is Reasonable From Answer With Truth: The Ambassador's Field Manual for Leading Your Family Spiritually:

- Jesus performed miracles to give evidence for who He is. - When Thomas doubted, Jesus showed him the scars in His hands (John 20:27). - The Bible records eyewitness accounts, historical events, and fulfilled prophecies ,  all evidence, not just feelings. Faith in Christ is based on evidence. As we will see as we go through this course together, there are well-documented historical facts that give evidence for the resurrection of Jesus. We trust Jesus because the evidence supports his claim to be the only way to salvation. Evidence Matters When someone asks, "Why are you a Christian?" and you answer, "Because it's true," their next question will likely be, "How do you know it's true?" That's when we can share the evidence that we will soon be learning of: - The Minimal Facts Argument - Reliability of the New Testament Manuscripts We'll dive into that evidence in the next lessons. But for now, remember: - Your why must be built on truth. - Feelings can change. Truth doesn't. Key Takeaways - Don't confuse how you became a Christian with why you are one today. - Feelings are personal and are subject to change. - The best (and only solid) reason to be a Christian because it's true.

- Christian faith is a reasonable faith that is based on evidence, not a blind faith. - Be ready to explain your why ,  clearly, simply, and confidently. Reflection Write your "why" in 1-2 sentences. - Avoid using "I feel" or describing an emotional event. - Focus on truth and evidence. Example: "I am a Christian because Christianity is true. The life, death, and resurrection of Jesus are supported by historical evidence, eyewitness testimony, and the reliability of Scripture."

LESSON 3: THE PURPOSE OF APOLOGETICS Learning Objectives. By the end of this chapter you will be able to: - Understand your identity and calling as Christ's ambassador - Explain the true purpose of Christian apologetics - Apply the first four tactics in spiritual conversations - Maintain a loving, respectful demeanor while defending truth Introduction: A New Identity "We are therefore Christ's ambassadors, as though God were making his appeal through us..." 2 Corinthians 5:20 Welcome to your new identity. You are not just a Christian, you are an ambassador of the Kingdom of Heaven, personally appointed by the King of Kings to represent Him in a foreign land. This identity changes everything about how you approach spiritual conversations. You're not just sharing your personal opinion or religious preference. You're representing the Truth Himself, and that comes with both incredible privilege and serious responsibility. The Ambassador's Calling - What Ambassadors Do In the political world, ambassadors have three primary responsibilities: - Know their sovereign - They understand their leader's character, policies, and desires - Represent accurately - They don't improvise or substitute their personal opinions for those of their King - Prepare for challenges - They study potential objections and prepare and train for how to respond As Christ's ambassadors, we have the same responsibilities:

- Know Jesus - Not just personally, but accurately according to Scripture - Represent Him faithfully - Communicate His teachings without distortion - Be prepared - Study objections and know how to respond accurately and with love The Preparation Problem Here's the sobering reality: most Christian ambassadors are completely unprepared. We've relegated apologetics to seminary professors and professional debaters, leaving ordinary believers defenseless against basic challenges to their faith. The result is predictable: - 70% of Christian teenagers abandon their faith in college - Many adults avoid spiritual conversations entirely because they don't want to look foolish - Christianity loses ground in the marketplace of ideas, not because it lacks evidence, but because its ambassadors are ineffective Your First Assignment Your first assignment as Christ's ambassador is your own family. Before you can effectively represent Christ to the world, you need to be able to lead your household spiritually. This means being prepared for topics such as: - Christianity might be your truth, but it's not my truth - I think all religions are valid paths to God - Only science yields truth, and religion is a matter of opinion - The Bible is full of errors and contradictions - Why would a good God allow pain and suffering?

Your family should see you as a resource when tough questions arise. Invite them to ask you their tough questions. Open the door to these conversations in your home. The Purpose of Apologetics What Apologetics IS The word apologetics comes from the ancient Greek word apologia, which means to make a defense. It is a legal term to build ones case through reasoning and logic. The apostle Paul uses this word in the book of Acts, and Peter uses it in 1 Peter 3:15. In both of these cases we see both Paul and Peter using sound reasoning to make their arguments in the defense of their faith. This shows us that apologetics is biblical, and that we are told to "always be prepared" to use it (1Peter 3:15). Apologetics is removing obstacles that prevent people from seriously considering Christianity. As Alister McGrath explains, "The goal of apologetics is not to win arguments or argue someone into Christianity. Apologetics is about removing the obstacles that are in people's way." Think of it this way: - Apologetics clears the path - Evangelism invites people to walk down the cleared path - They work together, not in competition Apologetics is not: - Apologizing - Trying to argue people into Christianity - Trying to display intellectual superiority Biblical Examples

Peter (Acts 2): Speaking to Jews, he used Old Testament references and Jewish expectations to show how Jesus fulfilled their hopes. Paul (Acts 17): Speaking to Greeks, he quoted their poets and connected to their "unknown God" rather than using Jewish references that would have been obstacles. Same gospel, different approach. Both apostles removed the specific obstacles in their audiences' paths. Evidence & The Minimal Facts Introduction We already established that we are not Christians because it "works for me" or because it makes us feel good. We are Christians because it is true. And we know that it's true because of the evidence. Christianity Is Built on Evidence - Jesus didn't ask for blind faith. The reason he performed miracles, fulfilled prophecies, and made post-resurrection appearances was to provide us with evidence. - Christianity is unique among religions ,  it rests on a single historical event: the resurrection of Jesus. - As Paul wrote in 1 Corinthians 15:14: "If Christ has not been raised, our preaching is useless and so is your faith." - It is important to understand that Christianity is not built on the teachings of Jesus or on the Bible. It is based on the historical resurrection of Jesus. - After three years of listening to teachings of Jesus, his disciples denied him and ran away.

- But after seeing him risen from the dead they began preaching boldly and changed the world. - The early Church had no Bible - it was still being written! What they had was eyewitnesses of the resurrection! The Minimal Facts Approach - Historical facts about Jesus that are accepted even by skeptical scholars. - Provides evidence for Christianity without even opening a Bible. - Demonstrates that Jesus' resurrection is the best explanation for the evidence. Our 5 minimal facts are: - Jesus of Nazareth died by Roman crucifixion. - He was buried in a well known private tomb. - His tomb was found empty soon after his internment. - His followers had experiences they believed were appearances of the risen Jesus. - Their lives were transformed by these experiences, leading to a willingness to die for their beliefs. 3 Bonus Facts: - James, Jesus' skeptical brother, converted after he claimed to see the risen Jesus. - Paul, (a former enemy of Christianity) converted after claiming to see the risen Jesus. - The Christian church has explosively grown into the most global, multicultural movement that the world has ever seen, despite the notoriously unpopular message of giving your money to the poor, sexual abstinence, and promised persecution. These facts are supported historically ,  even non-Christian scholars acknowledge them.

The best explanation is that Jesus actually rose from the dead. When you present these facts, you should conclude by asking if they have a better explanation for these historical facts. Four More Pieces of Evidence 1. Embarrassing Testimony If someone were making up a story, they would make themselves sound smart, loyal, and courageous. Yet throughout the New Testament its authors reveal quite a bit of embarrassing information about themselves. This is good evidence that the story is actually true: - The disciples were often unable to understand Jesus' parables. - Peter denied even knowing Jesus after he was arrested. - When Jesus asked his disciples to pray for him, they fell asleep instead. - Jesus actually called Peter "Satan" due to the way he was acting. This kind of embarrassing testimony is further evidence that the story is true. 2. Women's Testimony The New Testament authors tell us that women were the first witnesses of the resurrection in a culture where women's testimony wasn't considered reliable. A woman's testimony was not admissible in court in first century Judea. Yet, the authors tell us that women were the first to find the tomb empty, first to see the risen Jesus, and first to tell others the good news. They would not have said this unless it were true.

3. No Motive to Lie Jesus' followers were tortured and killed for proclaiming that Jesus was the Son of God and he had risen from the dead. They were stoned, crucified, beheaded, stabbed, and even flayed for their testimony. Yet none of them ever recanted. While it is true that many people are willing to die for what they believe, people do not die for something they know to be a lie. There is no other explanation as to why they would all be willing to die for saying Jesus rose from the dead, unless he really had! 4. A Terrible Sales Pitch If you were inventing a religion, you'd want to get as many followers as possible, so you would promise your followers things like wealth, land, power, and prestige. Maybe even multiple wives (does this sound familiar?). Christianity promises the opposite: - Jesus said that if you choose to follow him, the world will hate you (John 15:18-19) - Jesus told his followers to sell everything they owned and give the money to the poor (Matthew 19:21) - Jesus said for his disciples to deny themselves and take up their cross (Mark 8:34).

This is further evidence that the message of the gospel is not the invention of a human. No one would make up such an unappealing religion. Why This Matters Christianity doesn't just ask for belief ,  it invites investigation. When someone asks, "How do you know Christianity is true?" you can respond: - Because of the Evidence. - Historical evidence that even non-Christian scholars accept as fact - The Minimal Facts approach points to the resurrection. - And because even embarrassing and costly details support its truth. Key Takeaways - Christianity is true because of historical evidence. - The Minimal Facts argument is powerful because even skeptics agree with the data. - Embarrassing details and a "bad sales pitch" strengthen the case. - The best explanation of the evidence is that Jesus really rose from the dead. Reflection Imagine a friend asks you: "How do you know Christianity is true?" Write your answer in 3-4 sentences using the Minimal Facts.

section: Lesson 5: Reliability of the New Testament Manuscripts
text: Can We Trust the Bible We Have Today? Introduction One of the most common attacks on Christianity is: "The Bible has been copied so many times, and we don't have the originals. We can't know what it originally said." This lesson shows why that's incorrect. Christianity has more historical manuscript evidence than any other ancient writing. In fact, there are more writings about Jesus of Nazareth than about Julius Caesar - the emperor of Rome! But before we dive into our evidence for why we as Christians believe the New Testament is reliable, we need to first understand that the person who makes a claim is responsible to support their claim with evidence. That means that if someone says, "The Bible is full of errors and contradictions." Our response should be, "Really? Can you show me the ones that you've found?" The burden of proof is on the one who makes the claim. So, don't start jumping through hoops to dismantle an unsupported claim. They made the claim, and you should ask them to support it. If they are unable, you should encourage them to investigate their claim, and discover, as you have, that the New Testament is in fact reliable.

section: Lesson 5: Reliability of the New Testament Manuscripts
text: Now, we have just made a claim that we need to support. So, let's look at our evidence and see why the biographies of Jesus are reliable. The acronym MERET is used to help us remember 4 reasons why we trust the testimonies in the Bible. Multiple, Early, Recorded, Eyewitness Testimony Think of MERET as the building blocks of reliable history: - Multiple - More than one person recorded the events. The gospels aren't just one person's story; they're the accounts of multiple men! We have biographies and letters from men such as Paul, Peter, James, Jude, Mathew, Mark, Luke, and John. These multiple attestations create a very high degree of certainty in historical writings. To put this in perspective, only three ancient authors wrote an account of Julius Caesar. Additionally, other religions such as Islam and Mormonism rely on the testimony of one single man who was by himself when he claimed to have heard from God. The eight men who recorded their testimonies about Jesus are more sources than any other Therefore, the eight sources that we have for Jesus make the New Testament a very credible source.

section: Lesson 5: Reliability of the New Testament Manuscripts
text: - Early - Written within the lifetime of the eyewitnesses of Jesus' resurrection, not centuries later. Some creeds (like 1 Corinthians 15:3-5) date to within a few years of the crucifixion. To put this into historical perspective, the only two biographies that we have on Alexander the Great were written over 400 years after he lived. The accounts we have of Jesus are written while all the witnesses were still alive. That means the claims could be investigated and verified. There is no time here for legends to grow or for stories to be changed as they are passed from generation to generation. We literally have the men who fished, walked, and ate meals with Jesus doing the writing.

section: Lesson 5: Reliability of the New Testament Manuscripts
text: - Recorded - The New Testament manuscripts were not an oral history that was spread by word of mouth. God came to earth at such a time when men could record their accounts on papyrus or animal skins. Testimonies were written down, scribes meticulously copied, and manuscripts were circulated widely. By the Council of Nicaea in 325 AD, there were already many hundreds of manuscripts in Greek, Coptic, Latin, and Syriac circulating all over the Roman Empire, Asia Minor, Jerusalem, Greek Islands, Western Europe, and northern Africa. This widespread circulation of recorded manuscripts would render it impossible for anyone to change the original message. - Eyewitness - Many authors were eyewitnesses to the events. Matthew, John, Peter, Paul, and James are all authors of New Testament books who were right there with Jesus and knew him personally (James was actually the half-brother of Jesus). They spent several years with Jesus during his ministry; they saw him perform miracles, they saw him get arrested, and they saw him executed on a Roman cross. Then they saw him alive and well.

section: Lesson 5: Reliability of the New Testament Manuscripts
text: This makes Christianity's foundation far stronger than myths or legends, and far more historically reliable than those religions or movements that follow the words of just one man. God knows that we humans need to have evidence in order to have a reasonable faith in something. That is why he performed miracles and rose from the dead - so that we would have evidence that he is indeed the way, the truth, and the life. Differences vs. Contradictions

section: Lesson 5: Reliability of the New Testament Manuscripts
text: Some people point to the differences in details within the gospel accounts as a way to disparage the gospel message. Their thinking goes like this: if one writer says something different from another writer than nothing that either of them ever says can be trusted. This is of course completely ridiculous. For example, if your friends Bob, Tom, and Jim tell you about their weekend and Bob says, "We went to the lake and saw Nancy and Susan." Tom says, "We all went fishing and swimming and we ran into Susan and some others." And finally, Jim says, "We took the boat out and had a blast." Have your friends contradicted one another? No, of course not. Are there any errors in their stories? Nope. Actually, we have a fuller picture of what took place over the weekend. They clearly went fishing, swimming, and boating at the lake with Nancy, Susan, and some others, and they had a lot of fun.

section: Lesson 5: Reliability of the New Testament Manuscripts
text: In fact, if Bob, Tom, and Jim, all three said the exact same thing to you verbatim, you would get a little suspicious. It would sound like they had actually gotten together and all rehearsed what they would tell you. Every cop in the world knows that if all the witnesses say the exact same thing then they are lying. When people tell the truth, they tell the parts that they remember, that stood out to them. Let's take a look at some examples from the New Testament. - Difference = different details (Mary was there vs. Mary and Joanna were there). - Contradiction = one witness says the opposite of what another witness says. - Differences are expected from real eyewitnesses ,  detectives say exact agreement usually means collusion. Common "Contradictions" Skeptics Use - The Sign on the Cross - some gospels give a short form, others a longer version, John notes it was written in 3 languages. - Order of Jesus' Temptations - listed in different sequence, but both agree the temptations happened.

section: Lesson 5: Reliability of the New Testament Manuscripts
text: - Women at the Tomb - the four gospels give names of different women that found the tomb empty. But the logical conclusion is that ALL of those women were there, and no one author wrote a comprehensive list. This is a difference, not a contradiction. None of these are contradictions. They are differences of perspective and detail. Manuscript Evidence: An Embarrassment of Riches - Over 26,000 ancient manuscripts of the New Testament exist. - Over 5,000 in Greek (the original language). - By comparison, most other ancient works have only a handful: - Homer's Iliad - about 1,800 manuscripts (still far fewer than the NT). - Herodotus' Histories - about 100 manuscripts. - Julius Caesar's Gallic Wars - fewer than 10 manuscripts. If historians trust Homer, Herodotus, and Caesar as reliable based on small numbers of manuscripts, then the case for the New Testament is overwhelming. Not the Game of Telephone Skeptics sometimes compare the Bible to the game of telephone, where a message gets garbled as it's whispered down a line. That's a false analogy. The Bible was: - Written down - Copied carefully - Shared widely across regions and languages A better analogy is a cookie recipe: - If 99 copies say "1 teaspoon vanilla" and 1 says "1 tablespoon," it's easy to spot the mistake.

section: Lesson 5: Reliability of the New Testament Manuscripts
text: - If someone adds nuts, the odd copy stands out. With so many manuscripts to compare, the original wording is clear. Handling Additions Honestly A few passages are not in the earliest manuscripts: - The long ending of Mark (snake-handling passage). - The woman caught in adultery (John 8). Modern Bibles include these passages but mark them in italics or with notes, so readers know that these verses are not found in the earliest manuscripts. Nothing is hidden. Scholars are upfront about which sections are 100% certain and which are debated. Why It's Good We Don't Have the Originals If the originals survived, they could have been: - Altered ,  one small letter could change a doctrine. - Worshiped like relics. - By losing the originals and multiplying copies, God made His Word impossible to corrupt globally. - Thousands of copies in many languages across vast regions mean no single person or group could change it. More Reliable than Caesar We have more ancient written evidence for Jesus than we do for Julius Caesar ,  yet no one doubts Caesar existed. So why doubt Jesus? Key Takeaways - Differences are not contradictions. - The NT manuscripts are the best-attested documents of antiquity. - The Bible was copied and preserved, not whispered like a rumor.

section: Lesson 5: Reliability of the New Testament Manuscripts
text: - Scholars are transparent about additions ,  nothing is hidden. - God ensured His Word was preserved by spreading it widely. Reflection If someone told you, "The Bible is like the telephone game," how would you respond? Write 2-3 sentences explaining why that's not true, using examples from manuscript evidence. Example: "That's not true because the New Testament wasn't whispered from person to person like a rumor. It was written down, copied carefully, and shared widely. With thousands of manuscripts to compare, scholars can tell exactly what the originals said. It's more like copying a recipe thousands of times ,  if one copy is off, it's easy to spot."

section: Lesson 6: The Deity & Death of Jesus
text: Who Is Jesus, and Why Did He Die? That's the most important question in the world - Who Is Jesus? - Every religion respects Jesus: Muslims call him a prophet, Buddhists see him as enlightened, Hindus say he's a reincarnation of Krishna, and atheists call him a great teacher. - But Christianity is clear: Jesus was God in human form. - That's the simple, direct answer: Jesus was God, the Creator, who took on human form. Avoiding "Christianese" - Don't answer with vague phrases like "He's the Messiah, the Christ, the Son of God." - Those are true but confusing to someone without background knowledge. - The clearest answer: Jesus was God in human form. - State it simply, and then pause and let it sink in. Did Jesus Claim to Be God? In any conversation, whether it's with a Muslim, Hindu, or atheist, after we establish that they hold Jesus in high regard, we should ask them, "Do you know who Jesus said that he was?" Skeptics often say: "Jesus never claimed to be God." The truth: He did, many times. Here are several strong passages to keep in your "tool bag":

section: Lesson 6: The Deity & Death of Jesus
text: - Mark 14:62 - When asked if he was the Messiah, Jesus replied: "I am." He then called himself the Son of Man - a reference to Daniel 7, which the high priest recognized as a divine claim. That's why they called it blasphemy. - John 8:58 - "Before Abraham was, I am." Jesus claimed that he existed before Abraham - who had been dead for over 2,000 years. Plus, he used God's personal name ("I AM") from Exodus. - John 10:30 - "I and the Father are one." - John 14:9 - "Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father." - Matthew 12:8 - "The Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath." (placing Himself above the Ten Commandments). You should memorize one or two of these so that you can clearly and confidently share them with others. Additionally, here's how Paul described Jesus: Colossians 1:15 - "The Son is the image of the invisible God." Philippians 2:6-8 - "Though he was God, he did not think of equality with God as something to cling to. 7 Instead, he gave up his divine privileges; he took the humble position of a slave and was born as a human being.

section: Lesson 6: The Deity & Death of Jesus
text: When he appeared in human form, he humbled himself in obedience to God and died a criminal's death on a cross. And here's how John described Jesus: John 1:1, 14 - "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God...and the Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us." Then Why Did Jesus Have to Die? This is one of the most important questions an ambassador must be ready to answer clearly. If Jesus really was God, why did He die? Couldn't God simply forgive sins without a cross? Why Death Was Necessary The Bible gives a consistent answer: death is the payment for sin. - Romans 6:23 - "The wages of sin is death." - Hebrews 9:22 - "Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness." - From Genesis onward, sin and death are linked. When Adam and Eve sinned, death entered the world. In the Old Testament, animals were sacrificed again and again to cover sin. These sacrifices pointed to the ultimate Lamb of God ,  Jesus ,  who would die once for all. Justice and Love at the Cross

section: Lesson 6: The Deity & Death of Jesus
text: - Justice: God is perfectly holy and just. If He ignored sin, He would no longer be just. A judge who lets criminals go free without consequence is corrupt. - Love: God is also perfectly loving. Instead of punishing us, He took the punishment Himself in Jesus. At the cross, God's justice and love meet perfectly: - Justice was satisfied ,  sin was paid for. - Love was demonstrated ,  God Himself made the payment. Why God Couldn't Just Snap His Fingers - If God simply erased sin without punishment, He would no longer be just. - Sin always has a cost ,  someone must make the payment. - Example: If someone wrecks your car and you "forgive" them, you still absorb the cost of repair. - Likewise, God absorbed the cost of our sin at the cross. Why This Matters for the Gospel - Jesus' death was not an accident or a tragedy. - It was the central act of God's plan to save the world. - The resurrection proves that his death accomplished what he said it would ,  victory over sin and death. The Gospel in One Sentence God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God. 2 Corinthians 5:21 Key Takeaways

section: Lesson 6: The Deity & Death of Jesus
text: - Jesus is God in human form. - He clearly claimed divinity many times. - Memorize at least one verse where he does so (Mark 14:62, John 8:58, etc.). - Jesus' death was necessary ,  not arbitrary. The penalty for sin is death. - The cross shows both God's justice and His love. Reflection If a skeptic asks you, "Why did Jesus have to die? Couldn't God just forgive without the cross?" how would you answer? Write 2-4 sentences about why Jesus had to die for our salvation. Example: "God is perfectly just, which means He can't ignore sin without becoming corrupt. Romans 6:23 says the wages of sin is death, and Hebrews 9:22 says there is no forgiveness without the shedding of blood. That's why animal sacrifices were required in the Old Testament ,  they pointed forward to Jesus. On the cross, God's justice was satisfied, and His love was shown, because He took the punishment Himself."

The Three Big Buckets Most objections to Christianity fall into one of three categories. Once you understand these three "buckets" and know how to respond to each one, you won't be caught off guard. Bucket 1: Relativism "Christianity might be true for you, but it's not my truth." The Claim: There is no objective truth. You have your truth, I have mine. Everything is relative to personal or cultural perspective and someone's "lived experience." Why It Sounds Good: It appears tolerant, inclusive, and humble. It avoids conflict and makes everyone feel validated. The Problem: Relativism is self-defeating. When someone says "There is no absolute truth," they're making an absolute truth claim. When they say "You can't judge other cultures," they're judging cultures that do judge others. The Real Test: Ask relativists if they think genocide, child sex trafficking, or racism are objectively wrong. If they say yes, they've abandoned relativism. If they say no, they've revealed the moral bankruptcy of their position. Response Strategy: - "Is it true that there's no truth?" - "Are you saying things like racism and sex trafficking are just a matter of opinion?"

- "Is it true that 2+2=4, and that two hydrogen and one oxygen make water, and that the Nile River is in Egypt? So, you do believe in mathematical truth, scientific truth, and geographic truth. So, it's certainly possible that there are other truths as well, such as those of Jesus." Bucket 2: Pluralism "All paths lead to God" The Claim: All religions are basically the same, different paths up the same mountain to the same God. We just use different names and practices. Why It Sounds Good: It appears inclusive, peaceful, and respectful of all traditions. It avoids religious conflict and promotes harmony. The Problem: Pluralism violates the law of non-contradiction. Jesus made an exclusive truth claim, so logically speaking, all religions could be wrong, but they cannot all be true since Jesus claimed to be the only way to God: - Christianity: Jesus is the only way to God - Islam: Allah is the only true God, Jesus was just a prophet - Hinduism: There are many gods - Buddhism: Traditionally includes no gods at all These cannot all be true simultaneously.

Jesus's Exclusive Claims: "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). Either Jesus is the only way, or he's a liar, but he can't be "one of many good ways." Response Strategy: - "What did Jesus say about that theory?" - Jesus said, "wide is the gate and broad is the road that leads to destruction, and many enter through it. But small is the gate and narrow the road that leads to life, and only a few find it." (Matthew 7:13-14) - So, clearly Jesus did not think that all paths led to salvation. Bucket 3: Scientism "Only science yields truth" The Claim: Only science gives us real knowledge. Everything else, including religion, philosophy, and morality, is just opinion or superstition. Why It Sounds Good: It appears sophisticated, rational, and progressive. It aligns with our culture's respect for scientific achievement. The Problem: Scientism is self-defeating. The statement "Only science yields truth" is not itself scientific, it's a philosophical claim about science that cannot be proven through scientific experimentation. Science Cannot Explain: - Universal objective moral laws (Why is justice better than injustice?) - Logical truths (The law of non-contradiction) - Mathematical truths (the rationality of numbers) - Aesthetic truths (Why are we attracted to beauty, music, art & poetry?)

- The rationality of the universe is orderly and knowable (It appears to be designed - not random) Response Strategy: - "Is that statement itself scientific?" - "Can you prove through the scientific method that only science yields truth?" - "Can science tell us why love is better than hate?" - "Since science is the study of the natural world, it is incapable of addressing that which is supernatural, right?" Points to Remember: - Science itself depends on a designed, orderly universe that follows consistent laws. - The founders of modern science (Newton, Galileo, Bacon) believed in God. They studied creation because they believed it was rational and designed. - If our minds were random accidents, why trust our thoughts or experiments? We trust reason because our minds were designed by God. Always start with a clarifier: "What do you mean by science?" Often, people confuse science with philosophy about science. Key Takeaways - Relativism is self-refuting ,  truth can't belong to just one person. - Pluralism ignores Jesus' own claim to be the only way. - Scientism is self-refuting ,  science can't prove its own claim that it is the only source of truth. - Questions are powerful tools to help people see the cracks in these beliefs. Practical Application Exercises Exercise: Bucket Identification

Read each statement and identify which bucket it falls into: - "What's true for you isn't necessarily true for me." - "All paths lead to the same divine destination." - "Science has made religion obsolete." - "You can't judge other people's beliefs." - "Christians, Muslims, and Hindus all worship the same God." - "There's no evidence for anything supernatural." Answers: 1-Relativism, 2-Pluralism, 3-Scientism, 4-Relativism, 5-Pluralism, 6-Scientism Reflection If a friend told you, "All religions lead to the same God," how would you respond? Example: "What did Jesus say about that? It sounds nice, but Jesus said in John 14:6 that 'I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.' If Jesus is telling the truth, then other religions can't also be true paths to God. Either He was lying, He was crazy, or He really is Lord. Christians believe He is Lord because of the resurrection evidence."

Lesson Reading: Why Does God Allow Suffering? Introduction If you could ask God one question, most people would ask: "Why do you allow so much suffering and evil in the world?" This is the toughest challenge Christians face. Ambassadors need to be prepared ,  not with quick clichés, but with wisdom and compassion. There are two main categories to this question: - Emotional - Someone is hurting right now and struggling personally. - Logical - Someone is making a philosophical argument against God's existence. We must discern which category they are in before answering. This can be done by simply asking the person, "Is there something going on in your life right now that has you asking this?" 1. The Emotional Problem of Evil - When people are grieving or suffering, they don't need logic. They need compassion. - Ask: "Are you going through something right now?" - Listen to their story. Be present. Sometimes the best response is silence, a hug, or offering prayer. - Remember: "Nobody cares how much you know until they know how much you care." Offer Hope: - "I don't know why this is happening, but I do know there is a loving and powerful God, and in the end he will make things right." The Logical Problem of Evil

The logical challenge usually sounds like: "If God is all-loving, he would stop evil. If God is all-powerful, he could stop evil. Evil exists, therefore a loving, powerful God cannot exist." Response: - Fallen World: God created a world that was perfect and good, but we rebelled from God, and it is because of our sin that death, pain, and suffering entered the world. Our world is fallen and broken right now, but God has promised us that it won't stay this way forever. - Free Will: God chose to create a world in which we have limited free will. Sure, God could have created a world without any free will, where no one could choose to sin or hurt another human. But a world without free will would also be a world without love. God knew that He had to give us the freedom to choose to love him in order for the love to be real. If you merely program a robot to love you and it has no other choice, then it is not real love. But with free will also comes the freedom to choose evil. God decided to create a world where humans could choose love or choose evil.

- False Premise 1: It is a mistake to think an all-loving God would instantly remove all pain. Short-term suffering is sometimes necessary for long-term good. Think of a parent allowing a child to feel the pain of a life-saving injection or medical proceedure; they permit that temporary pain to save the child's life. In the same way, God may allow temporary suffering in our lives to produce a much greater, lasting good. - False Premise 2: It is a logical contradiction to say an all-powerful God should stop all evil while also giving humans free will. As we discussed, free will is what makes love possible. God cannot enforce goodness without removing our freedom. If He stopped us from ever choosing evil, He would be removing our free will, and with it, our ability to love. Tough Questions People Ask "What about my grandma who wasn't a Christian?" - Never guess where someone's loved one is. Only God knows hearts. - Respond with honesty: "I don't know. I know God made her, God loved her, and God is just." - Remind them God respects free will ,  God doesn't force people to spend eternity with Him if they spent their life rejecting Him. "What about people who never hear about Jesus?"

- Romans 1 and Psalm 19 teach that creation itself reveals God. No one is has an excuse. - God judges people based on what they had access to know. - Old Testament believers and children show that God provides paths for those unable to fully understand. - We trust God's justice and love. He is fair. "Why do bad things happen to good people?" - Trick answer: There are no good people. Romans 3:23 says all have sinned. - The only time a bad thing happened to a truly good person was Jesus; and he volunteered. - His suffering purchased salvation for us. Turning Objections into the Gospel The problem of evil opens the door to share the hope of Christ. - Romans 3:23 - All have sinned. - Romans 6:23 - The wages of sin is death. - Romans 5:8 - God demonstrates His love for us in that while we were sinners, Christ died for us. - John 3:16 - God loved the world and gave His Son, so that whoever believes has eternal life. These verses show that Jesus himself faced evil and suffering and turned it into the greatest act of love. Key Takeaways - First ask: Is this an emotional or logical problem? Is this personal and is the questioner grieving. Respond accordingly.

- Emotional Problem→ Show compassion and hope. - Logical Problem→ Explain sin, free will and love. - Avoid speculation about people's loved ones; trust God's justice. - The cross is proof that God brings ultimate good out of sin and suffering. Reflection If someone asked you, "Why would a loving God allow suffering?" How would you answer? Write 3-4 sentences like you would respond to this question. Example: "When people suffer, it doesn't mean God isn't loving. Just as a parent allows a painful shot for a child's good, God allows temporary pain for greater purposes. We live in a world broken by sin, but the cross shows that God brings the greatest good out of the greatest evil."

section: Lesson 9: How to Share the Gospel
text: The Easy Question After weeks of learning how to handle tough objections, sometimes someone lobs you the easiest one: "How do I become a Christian?" This is not the time for tactics or long debates. It's time to clearly and simply share the gospel. Start With the Bad News People need to know why they need saving before they can understand the good news. Ask: - "Are you perfect? No, me neither." - "Do you believe people should be punished for moral crimes?" - "Have you ever committed any moral crimes?" (lying, stealing, hurting others) The Bible says, "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Rom 3:23). It also says, "the payment for sin is death" (Rom 6:23). So, you and I both agree that we are both sinners, and according to God, we both deserve death. That's the bad news. ...But Here's the Good News Jesus paid for our sins when He died on the cross. - Romans 5:8 ,  "While we were still sinners, Christ died for us." - Romans 6:23 ,  "The wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus." We don't earn salvation. We accept it as a gift. What to Do The Bible is clear:

section: Lesson 9: How to Share the Gospel
text: - Romans 10:9 ,  "If you confess with your mouth, 'Jesus is Lord,' and believe in your heart that God raised Him from the dead, you will be saved." - John 3:16 ,  "For God so loved the world that He gave His one and only Son, that whoever believes in Him shall not perish but have eternal life." No works, no rituals. Just believe and confess. Practical Ways to Share If you're nervous, you can fall back on a simple "Romans Road": - Romans 3:23 ,  All have sinned. - Romans 6:23 ,  Sin leads to death - Romans 5:8 ,  Christ died for us. - Romans 10:9 ,  Believe and confess. - Romans 10:13 ,  "Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved." Another picture: Imagine a just judge whose son is guilty. The judge fines him, then pays the fine himself. That's justice and love together. That's what God did for us. Avoid Adding to the Gospel Don't pile on extras like "stop sinning first," "go to church," or "do good works." Salvation is through faith alone, by grace alone (Eph 2:8-9). Works and obedience come after salvation, not before. Be Ready, Be Clear

section: Lesson 9: How to Share the Gospel
text: Practice saying the gospel simply, in your own words. In real life, you may be asked: "How do I become a Christian?" Be ready to knock that slow pitch out of the park. Reflection If someone asked you, "How do I become a Christian?" How would you answer? Write your response in 3-4 sentences using clear, simple words. Example: "The gospel is simple: we've all sinned, and the punishment for sin is death. But God loves us and sent His Son, Jesus, to die for our sins and rise again. If you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised Him from the dead, you will be saved. Salvation is a free gift ,  it's not about earning it through works, but trusting in Christ alone. That's how you become a Christian."

Lesson Reading: How to Use Questions Like an Ambassador Introduction Many Christians feel nervous talking about their faith. What if the other person is smarter? What if they ask a question we can't answer? The good news: you don't need to have all the answers. You only need to know how to ask great questions. Here's What Asking Tactical Questions Can Do for You: - Help you understand what someone means. - Put the burden of proof back on the person making the claim. - Create opportunities to share the gospel without being pushy. - Get the other person to consider their own beliefs. Ambassadors ask questions not to win arguments, but to represent Christ wisely, gently, and truthfully. Why Questions Work From Answer with Truth: - Statements can make people defensive. Questions invite them to think for themselves. - Questions lower tension and help you avoid "preaching." - Questions uncover hidden assumptions and weak spots in reasoning. - Questions buy you time if you feel overwhelmed. Four Great Tactical Questions 1) "What do you mean by that?" - Use this to clarify terms and uncover hidden assumptions. - Don't use it sarcastically or when you already know what they mean. - Example 1: - "I don't believe in God." - Response: "Who do you mean when you say God?"

- Maybe they imagine a bearded man on a cloud. You can explain the biblical God instead. - Example 2: - "I think all world religions are the same." - Response: "What do you mean by that?" - Allow them to fully unpack that worldview before you engage them on pluralism. - This prevents you from "defusing a bomb" before they've even built it. 2) "How did you come to that conclusion?" - Shifts the burden of proof back to the other person. - Helps reveal if their view has real evidence or just opinion. - Examples: - "Jesus never really died on the cross." → "How did you come to that conclusion?" - "Christianity copied other religions." → "What sources led you to that belief?" - "The Bible is full of errors and contradictions." → "How did you come to that conclusion?" - This question often exposes that they have nothing solid to back up their claim. As Greg Koukl (author of Tactics) says: "The person who makes the claim has the burden to give the reasons." 3) "Have you ever considered…?" - Gently introduces a new idea without being pushy. - Example 1: - "I think miracles are impossible." - Response: "Have you ever considered that if God exists, miracles would be possible?" - Example 2:

- "I don't believe that a man came back from the dead." - Response: "Have you ever considered the historical evidence for it?" - Think of this as "planting a pebble in their shoe" ,  a thought they carry away and wrestle with later. 4) "Hypothetically, if Christianity were true, would you become a Christian?" - Reveals whether a person is actually open to truth or just closed off. - If they say yes → they are genuinely seeking. Share the evidence. - If they say no, even if it were true I wouldn't believe → they are not seeking truth. - You can thank them for their honesty and move on. - This also works both ways: you should be ready to say yes if another worldview were proven true. Christians are committed to pursuing truth wherever it leads ,  and the evidence leads to Jesus, who claimed to be the truth. Putting Questions into Practice A. Everyday Scenarios - At work: Someone says, "The Bible is full of fairy tales." → "Do you believe that miracles are possible?" - With friends: "I think you can have your truth and I can have mine." → "How did you come to that conclusion? Have ever considered the truths of math, science, history, or geography?"

- With skeptics online: "All religions are the same." → "Have you ever considered what Jesus Himself said about being the only way?" B. Tactical Reminders - Always ask with respect, not sarcasm. - Listen carefully to the answer. Don't just wait for your turn to speak. - Be willing to let the conversation end with a question or by giving them the last word ,  you don't have to close every discussion with a "mic drop." Why This Matters for Ambassadors - Questions shift the focus from debating to dialogue. - They show humility: you don't claim to have all the answers. - They invite people to process truth themselves. - Most importantly, they make conversations about Christ more natural and less confrontational. Remember: We are not trying to win arguments. We are trying to win people. Key Takeaways - Questions are powerful tools for ambassadors of Christ. - The four tactical questions: - "What do you mean by...?" - "How did you come to that conclusion?" - "Have you ever considered…?" - "Hypothetically, if Christianity were true, would you become a Christian?" - Use questions to clarify, challenge, and invite ,  never to mock. - Plant seeds of truth and let God do the growing. Reflection

Which tactical question do you think would be most helpful in your conversations, and why? Write 3-4 sentences with an example of how you might use it. Example: "I would use the question 'How did you come to that conclusion?' because it puts the responsibility back on the other person to provide evidence. If someone told me 'Jesus never rose from the dead,' I could calmly ask them where they got that idea. Many times they can't support it, and it opens the door for me to explain the historical evidence. This keeps the conversation respectful and thoughtful."

Men's Alliance is a discipleship ministry for Christian men who want more. We challenge men physically and spiritually through free outdoor workouts and real-world devotions. What Men's Alliance Is Not Men's Alliance is not a Bible study. We don't open a book and walk through verses chapter by chapter. We use scripture, but the format is built around brotherhood and discipleship, not academic study. Men's Alliance is not a workout group. The workout is the entry point. The devotion is the point. If a man comes for the workout and leaves before the devotion, he's missed the whole reason we exist. If a man wants a Bible study or a workout group, there are plenty of options in every city. A Tribe is a rare thing. Don't let it drift into something it was never designed to be.

Sharpening men to become the husbands, fathers, and leaders God designed us to be. Motto Tribal. Rugged. Real. Tribal, because the Enemy wants to keep us isolated and alone. He will attack us when we're away from our group. Rugged, because the Enemy wants to keep us comfortable and asleep on the sideline. And getting in the Game means rejecting comfort as the ultimate goal. Real, because the Enemy wants us to wear a facade like we're fine. Lying to each other like this just makes us feel more isolated. We admit our failures and weaknesses, because that's where actual living begins. MA Bible Verse "As iron sharpens iron, so one man sharpens another." Proverbs 27:17

I am a common man with an uncommon desire to succeed. I will keep myself physically strong, spiritually awake, and morally straight. I expect to lead and be led, and in the absence of leadership I will take charge and lead by example. I am not too proud to ask for help. I belong to a tribe, and do not struggle alone. My past failures do not define me. When the world knocks me down, I will get back up every time. Faithfulness to family and devotion to God will be my legacy. I will finish strong.

Values create culture. Values are our rudder. Honor - Show respect to everyone, especially to a Tribesman who is your elder. Do not lie, cheat, steal, or laugh at sin. Encouragement - Build up your fellow Tribesmen with words that are positive and hopeful. Do not break others down through sarcasm or insults. Be solution-focused, not problem-focused. Always be optimistic. Never give up hope. No matter what happens you can always say, ["Good."](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IdTMDpizis8) Trust - Believe the best about your Tribesmen. Give them the benefit of the doubt. Feedback - Seek feedback from others. Ask what your blindspots are. Thank people for their feedback. Become comfortable receiving feedback from others. Humility - Everyone has something to teach. Each Tribesman knows something that you don't know. It's better to under sell and over deliver. Perseverance - Our culture is one of finishing strong. Whether in a workout, or in our lives, we strive to leave a legacy of never quitting, of getting back up every time. You are never out of the fight.

The 12 Rules of Men's Alliance (in order). Rule 1: Don't die. Don't push yourself beyond your physical limits during the workout. If you need a break, take one. Rule 2: No women allowed. Men's Alliance is only for biological men ages 13 and up. Rule 3: Listen more than you talk. Share what's on your mind, and then be quiet and listen to others. Nobody likes a blatherskite. Rule 4: Be real. If you've lived for more than 3 decades then you've probably made some big mistakes. Maybe some REALLY big mistakes. This is not the kind of group where you pretend that you're a saint. Rule 5: Call each other out. A friend who lets me believe my own lies is no friend at all. Rule 6: Meet up. Get together outside of the weekly Tribe meeting. Add your tribe to your contacts on your phone. What kind of tribe can't even reach each other? Rule 7: Start and end on time. Our time is our most precious resource. Respect it. Rule 8: Show up every week and bring it! We don't do anything halfway in Men's Alliance. Whatever we do, we do it heartily (Col 3:23). So, if you're in this Tribe then commit to it. Rule 9: Walk the line. While all men are welcome to attend Men's Alliance, do not expect to lead if you're openly embracing a lifestyle that the New Testament condemns. Rule 10: Pray for your Tribe! Every tribe needs a prayer team to show up early each week to pray. Pray for God to bless our efforts, to anoint our benches and fire with His presence, and to mold us into better men. Rule 11: Modify workouts as needed. If you can't do a certain exercise, no problem. Simply do something else instead. Doing anything is better than doing nothing. Rule 12: What's said at the fire, stays at the fire. Things shared in a devotion are confidential. [See our 10 Rules Video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jSBDSHxZti4)

Men's Alliance is an utterly unique ministry for Christian men. We use three levels of training (Callsign, Coin, and Patch) to build Brotherhood, Leadership, and Discipleship. There is nothing currently offered in churches similar to this. And to earn the MA Patch, a man must learn how to clearly give the Gospel, and how to answer tough questions about what Christians believe and why. At Men's Alliance, every man is a leader. We take turns leading workouts and devotions, and we create opportunities for men who have never led anything in their life to develop the leadership skills necessary to effectively lead their families spiritually. Workout groups and Bible studies are everywhere. But a Tribe is a very rare thing. 3 Levels of Training - Callsign (Brotherhood) - is a man who has attended a minimum of five (5) Tribe meetings and whose Tribe Leader has determined that he is ready to be given a callsign by the Tribe. He is eligible to display the MA Shield on his vehicle. He is also now eligible to lead a workout and devotion and start a fire. - Coin (Leadership) - After becoming a Tribesman, we want men to become leaders. We encourage and equip men to lead a workout and devotion, build a fire, and learn our creed. We recognize leadership through our MA Challenge Coin, which is carried daily as a reminder of who we are. Earning the MA Challenge Coin is a significant event. It is an honor to carry it, and live out the creed each day. - Patch (Discipleship) - is the highest level of Men's Alliance training. It signifies that a man has devoted himself to living out the Men's Alliance creed. He is a leader both in his home and around the fire. Most importantly, the patch indicates that a man knows how to give the Gospel of Jesus Christ to others, and can answer their questions about what Christians believe and why. To earn the MA Patch, a Coined Member must have completed [Start The Fire](https://startthefire.org), the MA Online Ambassador Training Course and then sign up for Carry the Fire on the Men's Alliance website.

section: THE FLOW OF A MEN’S ALLIANCE TRIBE
text: Every MA Tribe will follow the same flow, memorize the same verses, and recite the same creed. If you are from Alpha Tribe in Virginia, USA and you visit Braveheart Tribe in Budapest, Hungary it should feel just like home. From start to finish, every tribe should flow in this order. - Leader shows up 30 minutes early to build the fire and pray for the Tribe that's about to meet. - Welcome everyone to Men's Alliance. If you see any newbies, be sure to tell them to pace themselves during the workout. Rule #1 of Men's Alliance is "Don't die." - WORKOUT - Gather around the fire - Introductions - Randomly call on a callsign/coin/patch member to say the monthly memory verse. - If they don't know it, they owe the Tribe burpees (Callsign=5, Coin=10, Patch=15). - Repeat until someone gets it correct. - Announcements - Opening prayer - DEVOTION - Closing prayer - Callsign/Coin ceremony if needed - Huddle around the fire, recite the creed together (this signifies getting off the sidelines and into the game!) Why We Workout First The workout comes first for a reason. It's not filler, it's the icebreaker that helps us shed off the stress we brought and open our minds in preparation for the devotion. Hard physical effort breaks down walls between men. After 30 minutes of suffering side by side, a guy who would never open up in a church small group may tell you what's actually going on in his life. Sweat builds trust faster than conversation. The workout also tells the world what we are. We're not a Bible study with a coffee table. We're men who train, then turn to scripture. That order signals to every man who shows up that this is a different kind of men's group. Lead the workout like it counts. Skipping it, shortening it to make more devotion time, or treating it as warm-up changes the model and the erodes the benefit.

section: HOW TO BUILD A MEN’S ALLIANCE FIRE
text: There are a lot of things we don't do at Men's Alliance. We don't have refreshments, singing, or PowerPoint slides. The one thing we do is build fires, so let's make them great ones. No one wants to arrive at Men's Alliance two minutes before it starts and see a small, pathetic fire struggling to exist. When a man arrives at Men's Alliance, they should see a big blazing fire welcoming them to the Tribe. Tribe Leaders should ensure that someone is assigned to build the fire and that person should arrive 30 minutes early with fire-starting materials on hand. Tribe Leaders must ensure that someone is assigned to extinguish the fire. Put the fire out with more water than you think is necessary. Every Tribe Leader must ensure there is a bucket of water nearby before lighting the fire. Don't be the tribe that makes the evening news. And never, ever, ever, use gasoline to start a fire!

section: HOW TO LEAD A MEN’S ALLIANCE WORKOUT
text: - Make it intense, fast. We don't have time to mess around. Start on time and dive right in, elevate the heart rate, and make everyone sweaty, exhausted, and feeling the burn in a hurry. Given our short format, we can't afford to ease into the workout with 5 minutes of stretching. - Make it harder than you want it to be. You should look at your workout plan and think, "I'm not even sure if I can do this." If an NFL running back shows up at your tribe, we want him thinking, "Man, that workout was no joke." - Make it memorable. Dumbbell curls and pushups are completely forgettable, and we can all do those at home. So, make your MA workout memorable with things like flipping tires, swinging sledgehammers, bear crawling, dragging sandbags and sled races, because we want our workouts to be unforgettable. - Make it flexible by accommodating those who can't do certain things. We can't go for a 3-mile run down the street because that would leave folks behind. So make sure it accommodates those with disabilities, bad knees, and bad backs. Always tell people to go at their own pace and just do what they can. Each workout is just you against you. - No dodgeball, kickball, yoga or meditation. It's a workout, not a P.E. class or a women's group at the YMCA. Our website promises a rugged workout, so let's deliver that. If in doubt, ask your Tribe leader. - No explicit music. Making a workout playlist, bringing a speaker, and rocking out during the workout is highly encouraged, just make sure the music is clean. - Don't use a cross as a piece of workout equipment. The cross of Jesus is a sacred symbol of how much God loves us, and it's a reminder of the price Jesus paid for our salvation. Using it as a piece of workout equipment demeans it and sends a confusing message to those looking on. (In Luke 9:23 Jesus is speaking metaphorically on the cost of discipleship). A major element of Men's Alliance workouts is that they are difficult. We purposefully set the bar high, but at the same time we give grace to those who fall short. (This is how we should be in every aspect of our lives). We have some men who are tremendously fit, and others who are barely able to workout at all. We have found that it is much better to create the workout for the most fit person and then give others permission to do less. If we lower the standard to the least fit person then we are no longer "iron sharpening iron." After a man leads his first workout or devotion, he earns a leadership wrist band and should be presented with one by the Tribe Leader during the announcements around the fire. Disability and Limitation Inclusion Modification of exercises covers most cases. Some men need more than that. If a man can't do the workout at all because of a disability, injury, or medical condition, he's still welcome. Welcome him into the Tribe the same way you'd welcome anyone else. Find him a role: counting reps, timing rounds, holding the fire, leading the prayer. He's a Tribesman, not a spectator. If a man is hearing impaired, vision impaired, or has any other condition that affects how he participates in the workout or the devotion, ask him directly what he needs. Most men will tell you exactly what works and what doesn't. Callsign and Coin progression apply the same way. A man earns his callsign by showing up consistently and being known to the Tribe. He earns his coin by leading. A man who can't lead a workout because of a disability can still lead a devotion, build the fire, or run prayer. There's always a way. The standard is the standard. We don't lower it. But we make room for a man to meet it through whatever path his body allows.

section: HOW TO LEAD A MEN’S ALLIANCE DEVOTION
text: - Keep it short. Instead of planning a 30-minute devotion, plan a 15-minute devotion with 15 minutes of discussion around some big, open-ended questions. - Don't read it. It's ok to look down at notes to remind yourself of your main points and Bible verses, but don't just read the entire thing. - Make it personal. Talk about your own personal struggles, triumphs, and experiences. That's what connects people and makes it real. - Ask questions. Be a facilitator not a preacher. Men's Alliance is awesome because of the sharing done by each man around the fire. If you don't ask questions people won't share. - Embrace the silence. After you ask a question, be quiet. Even when the silence starts to get really awkward, resist the urge to start talking again, because somebody is about to share. - Don't discuss divisive topics. Prophecy, healing, speaking in tongues, and eschatology might be great topics for Sunday School, but not for Men's Alliance. - Don't bring church outside. We don't sing, baptize, or take communion at MA. If men want those things they can get them in church. Our goal is to offer something completely different, not more of what's already available every Sunday morning. - Don't speak "Christianese." We want men who are not Christians and who don't even like church to love Men's Alliance. This ain't a Sunday School class. - Send a heads up for adult content. There's absolutely nothing wrong with discussing mature adult topics related to sexual struggles, pornography addiction, etc. at Men's Alliance. In fact, that's what we're here for. However, you need to give everyone a heads up via GroupMe at least one day prior so that those who bring their younger sons can make arrangements to not bring them that week. If a young boy (under 15) is present, please talk around the adult content instead of being explicit. - Adhere to The Essentials of Our Faith. Below is the MA Essentials of Our Faith. In order to lead a devotion, you must agree with each and every element of it. If you have any questions about it or you're not sure what something means, please reach out to info@mensalliancetribe.com If anything is said at Men's Alliance that goes against what the Bible says, then it should be corrected immediately using scripture. We do not want anyone to walk away thinking that we condone teaching that is contrary to the Bible. Pictures are great, but videos of Devotions should never be recorded or shared in any way. After a man leads his first workout or devotion, he earns a leadership wrist band and should be presented with one by the Tribe Leader immediately after completing the workout or devotion. Special Guests Leading Workouts or Devotions The answer is no. There are no exceptions. Leadership at Men's Alliance is earned through the Tribe. A man leads a workout or devotion after he's been in the Tribe, earned his callsign, and is known to the men around the fire. Visiting pastors, friends of the Tribe, and guest speakers do not lead. They are welcome to attend, sweat, and share around the fire like any other man. They do not take the workout or the devotion.

All people have sinned and fallen short of the glory of God. (Romans 3:23) The penalty for sin is death. (Romans 6:23) God, the Creator of the Universe, eternally existing in three persons (Father, Son, and Holy Spirit), came to earth as the person of Jesus. Jesus is God in human form. (Colossians 1:15) Jesus was born of a virgin, lived a sinless life, and willingly died on a Roman cross to pay the death penalty for our sins. He rose from the dead on the third day just as he predicted. (1 Corinthians 15:3-8) Salvation is a free gift that is available to everyone. We are saved by grace and not by our good works or any human efforts. (Ephesians 2:8-9) "If we confess with our mouth that Jesus is Lord, and believe in our hearts that God raised him from the dead we will be saved." (Romans 10:9) Jesus made the powerful claim to be the only way to God the Father. This means that Jesus is either the only way or he is a liar, but having made such a claim, he logically cannot be just a good teacher. (John 14:6) The Bible is our blueprint for how we should live and what we believe. When faced with conflicting ideas from culture or personal feelings, we trust Scripture to guide us in truth. (Psalm 119:105) Who Must Agree to the Essentials of Our Faith Any man who leads a devotion must agree to the Essentials of Our Faith in full. This is non-negotiable. Any man in a formal leadership role, Tribe Leader, Workout Chief, Devotion Chief, Prayer Chief, Fire Chief, Regional Leader, Division Leader, Board Member, employees, or Officer, must also agree to the Essentials of Our Faith and sign the Leadership Agreement. A callsign member who has not yet led a devotion and is not in a formal role is welcome around the fire regardless of where he is in his faith. He doesn't have to sign anything. We meet men where they are. Leading is a different threshold.

section: THE 12 BIBLE MEMORY VERSES OF MA
text: Each month, every Tribesman in Men's Alliance will memorize the same Bible verse together. At each Tribe gathering one callsign member will be called on at random to recite that month's memory verse. If he is unable to do so, he must do 5 burpees (or substitute for a different exercise if he is unable to do burpees). - January - Romans 3:23 - February - Romans 6:23 - March - Romans 5:8 - April - Romans 10:9 - May - Romans 10:13 - June - John 3:16 - July - Colossians 1:15 - August - Ephesians 2:8-9 - September - John 14:6 - October - Acts 4:12 - November - John 16:33 - December - Hebrews 12:1-2 Tribesmen will only be tested on the current month's memory verse. In addition to the 12 Bible memory verses of MA, there are also 12 Patched memory verses for Patched Tribesmen who want to learn more. It is highly encouraged that Patched Tribesmen commit these verses to memory; however, there is not a burpee penalty for not knowing them. The 12 Patched Memory Verses - January - 2 Corinthians 5:21 - February - Hebrews 9:22 - March - Galatians 2:20 - April - 1 Peter 3:15 - May - Romans 12:2 - June - 2 Corinthians 5:20 - July - John 14:9 - August - 2 Corinthians 10:5 - September - Mark 8:34-36 - October - 2 Corinthians 5:17 - November - Romans 5:3-4 - December - Acts 20:24

We give callsigns to each other to build camaraderie and brotherhood. A callsign shows that you belong to a Tribe. It helps us get to know each other better and creates a shared tribal culture. Here are the rules for callsigns: - You have to come at least five times to the same Tribe in order to get a callsign. But you're not guaranteed a callsign on your fifth time. You'll get it whenever the Tribe Leader feels that you've earned it. - You have veto power over any callsign that you don't like. You only get one veto. - Every callsign must have a story/meaning behind it. Example: callsign Fresh because you work in produce, Rabbit because you're fast, or Flip because you flip houses. You can't just pick an arbitrary callsign because you like it. - It has to be a callsign that you can yell across a crowded public area without embarrassing yourself. - It cannot be a callsign that a 7th grader would like (Ironman, Superman, Batman, etc). The best callsigns are simple, not cool (Router, Bench, Hops, etc.) - Callsigns must not be demeaning (like Slug or Tard). A callsign should call out greatness and give you something to live up to (like Sage, Guardian, Condor, or Phoenix). - To give an official Men's Alliance callsign to someone, they must stand up in front of the Tribe at the end of a weekly meeting and be able to say the Men's Alliance motto and verse (Proverbs 27:17). If they cannot do this, then they will not receive their callsign that week. Then they introduce themselves and tell some interesting facts about themselves (hobbies, job, where they're from, etc.). Then callsign suggestions are shouted out by the other men. - The Tribe Leader puts the best suggestions to a vote and the callsign is conferred on the new member. - To make it official, the new member must sound their loudest, fiercest war cry of "Tribe!" We live in a culture that tells men to be quiet, and where our strength is not always appreciated. At Men's Alliance we want to bring out the God-given warrior heart in each man. - The new callsigned tribesman is then prayed for by the Tribe and receives a MA decal from the Tribe Leader. - We won't force anyone to get a callsign. If a man wants to come to MA more than five times but is not ready to officially join and receive a callsign, that is fine. He can continue to "audit" Men's Alliance until he's ready. - It is up to the Tribe Leader and Chiefs to decide when a man is ready to get his callsign. This may take more than just five weeks. No one is entitled to a callsign just because they showed up five times.

When a man is given a callsign he is also immediately given a wingman, before he even gets off the tire. The wingman should be a seasoned member of the Tribe who attends regularly and can help get the new Tribesman connected to GroupMe, the roster, [The War Cry](https://mailchi.mp/mensalliancetribe/b2yy6tyhdx), the [Men's Alliance Podcast](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLvRuoy83AIF8yVUXzuhf6z1Lgcl1OvdoE), and answer all of his questions about Men's Alliance. The wingman should have regular contact with the new Tribesman, meeting for lunch or coffee, etc. Most importantly, the wingman's job is to make sure the new guy feels like he belongs to the Tribe. And if the day ever comes when the new guy doesn't show up, it is the wingman's job to find out why and help out if there's a problem. After three months of having a wingman, the new Tribesman is no longer new. He can now become a wingman for others and attempt to earn his challenge coin.

section: HOW THE CHALLENGE COIN IS EARNED
