text: The challenge coin is carried in your pocket daily as a reminder of who you are and who you want to be. It reminds you that you belong to a tribe and you do not struggle alone. It reminds you to finish strong. - Challenge coins cannot be earned until the Tribesman has led a workout and a devotion, and built a fire, and his Tribe Leader agrees that he is ready. - Challenge coins are earned by standing on the tire and reciting the Creed in front of the Tribe. - The Tribesman must let his Tribe Leader know in advance that he would like to earn his coin. - The Tribe must be completely silent and not assist in any way. - If the Tribesman messes up any line, he will be immediately stopped by the Tribe Leader and he will be allowed one chance to start over from the beginning. If a second mistake is made, the individual will have to wait until the next coin ceremony to try again. The Challenge Coin Rules: If a Coined Member meets another Coined Member in a public establishment such as a restaurant, coffee shop, hospital, etc. (not during a weekly Tribe meeting) he can challenge them by putting his coin on the table or bar. If any Coined Member cannot produce his own coin in response, he must do 10 burpees on the spot (15 burpees for Patched Members) or buy a beverage for the man who started the challenge. However, if all Coined Members present are able to respond with their coin, then the man who initiated the challenge must do the burpees or buy them all a beverage of their choice.

The MA Patch indicates a man who is both a leader of men and an ambassador for Christ. Earning your patch consists of training in both small-unit leadership skills and practical apologetics. A Patched Member is a man who can answer common questions about Christianity and give the Gospel clearly. In order to earn the MA Patch, a man must complete [Start the Fire](https://startthefire.org), an online interactive course, Carry the Fire, which is an intensive course consisting of eight weeks of virtual training culminating in an unforgettable Field Training Exercise (FTX) designed to make a man a better leader and a better ambassador for Christ. While the specific details of the FTX are not given beforehand, rest assured that earning your MA Patch is a day you will remember for the rest of your life. To register for Carry the Fire, a man must have earned his MA Challenge Coin and completed Start the Fire. Minimum age for Carry the Fire is 15 years old. Registration for Carry the Fire is linked upon completion of [Start the Fire](https://startthefire.org).

Tribe Leader Each Tribe has one Tribe Leader who ensures the Tribe is following this Leadership Guide each week. The Tribe Leader is also responsible for making the decision to cancel or not if weather is inclement. Additionally, the Tribe Leader should attend the MA Leadership meetings and make sure his Tribe is up to date with the latest MA podcasts, newsletters, website info, etc. The Tribe Leader is expected to earn his Challenge Coin and Patch within 6-12 months of stepping into the position. Workout & Devotion Chiefs Each Tribe must also have a Workout Chief and Devotion Chief whose job is to make sure that someone is signed up each week to lead, and to ensure that the workout and devotion will meet the MA guidelines. Every week the Chiefs must check with whoever is signed up to lead and ask them what their workout plan is and what their devotion topic will be. This will help prevent bad workouts and inappropriate devotions from occurring. Following tribe, the Chiefs should debrief the tribesman who led to help them to grow and continue to improve. Prayer Chief Each Tribe needs to designate a Prayer Chief who can pray strategically for the needs of each individual Tribe and the Men's Alliance ministry. Fire Chief The Fire Chief is responsible for ensuring someone is always signed up to build the fire and to make sure the Tribe has sufficient firewood. Finance Chief (optional) Tribes that take up local donations or run local fundraisers should designate a Finance Chief. The Finance Chief tracks money in and money out for the local Tribe, keeps simple records, and works with the Tribe Leader to make sure spending matches what was raised for. The Finance Chief is not a CPA. He doesn't need accounting credentials. He needs to be trustworthy, organized, and willing to keep a log. Local Tribe funds are not tax-deductible (see Finance FAQ in Appendix 5). Only donations made to Tip of the Spear Ministries through the official MA giving channels are tax-deductible. Make sure your donors know the difference before they give. Social Chief (optional) Meeting up outside the weekly workout and devotion is an important part of building a band of brothers and creating a Tribe. The Social Chief is responsible for setting up events such as lunches, family picnics, camping trips, etc. for tribesmen to connect with each other. Media Chief (optional) A picture is worth a thousand words. The Media Chief is responsible for taking great pictures and sharing them on social media. Social media is a great way to engage with others and promote your Tribe. Please review our Social Media Guide in Appendix 5 for important information about maintaining the DNA of Men's Alliance in what you post. Wingman Chief (optional) Each Tribe should have one Wingman Chief. The Wingman Chief makes sure all new call sign members are assigned a wingman who will get them connected with GroupMe, Leadership sign up, the Leadership Guide, and the next steps of training (coin and patch). Regional Leader Above the Tribe Leader, there is a Regional Leader. The Regional Leader's main job is to ensure that Tribe Leaders have the DNA of Men's Alliance. Division Leader Above the Regional Leader is the Division Leader. The Division Leader's main job is to ensure that Regional Leaders have the DNA of Men's Alliance.

We don't want anyone burning out due to leading at Men's Alliance, and sometimes we all need a break from leadership positions. Tribe Leaders are encouraged to hand over leadership about every 18 months or as needed, and Workout/Devotion Chiefs are encouraged to hand over leadership every one year or as needed. We should all be training our replacements every day, and building up new leaders who can take over as Tribe Leaders and Chiefs. If your Tribe can't live without you, then you're doing it wrong. All MA Leaders (Tribe Leaders, Chiefs, Region/Division Leaders, Board of Directors, and Officers) are required to sign a Men's Alliance Leadership Agreement. This Agreement commits everyone in a leadership position to adhere to our Essentials of Our Faith, Honor Code, and Leadership Guide. Before taking a leadership position for the first time, update your profile on the website. By doing so you will be prompted to digitally sign the Leadership Agreement. (See Appendix 1) How to Digitally Sign the Leadership Agreement Complete the Leadership Agreement signing form before taking your leadership position for the first time. Sign here:[ ](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSdn8ur-bhlllr3wKQrFKiNeAuaUQiiq98Zu8LcySyY5UwbN2Q/viewform)[Leadership Agreement Signing Form](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSdn8ur-bhlllr3wKQrFKiNeAuaUQiiq98Zu8LcySyY5UwbN2Q/viewform)

The minimum age for attending Men's Alliance is 13 years old. In the past we allowed boys of much younger ages to join Men's Alliance because we want to encourage fathers and sons to attend together. However, we have learned that it is best if a young man is at least 13 years of age. This also allows younger boys to view Men's Alliance as something they get to attend when they are "old enough." It gets them excited about turning 13, and it associates in their mind that Men's Alliance is a big deal, not for little kids. We want young men to look forward to being old enough to join MA with their dads.

Every Tribe is required to complete one community service project per year. The Tribe Leader should first check with the pastor of the church the Tribe is affiliated with to see if the church has anything that they need done. If the church does not, then the Tribe should look to the local community. The project will be organized and funded with donations from the local Tribe. Additionally, Tribes may choose to volunteer for our Helping the Heroes community service. Helping the Heroes provides gift cards for groceries to first responders each December. If a Tribe would like to assist with Helping the Heroes, they should contact info@mensalliancetribe.com SOCIAL MEDIA & MERCH While Men's Alliance uses social media to help share our vision, we will avoid topics that are unnecessarily divisive. If it wouldn't make for a suitable devotion topic, then it shouldn't be posted on MA social media either. Men's Alliance social media posts will focus on the MA mission and motto. This includes posting things of a political nature in GroupMe. All Men's Alliance Tribe accounts must have standardized names on social media. If your tribe has an Instagram or Facebook account please name it according to this format: MA_DamascusTribe, MA_KeelTribe, MA_DeltaTribe, MA_BMoreAlphaTribe, etc. All Tribes must use their official Tribe logo on their merch, and social media pages. Some tribes have older logos that they made themselves and these are no longer permitted with the use of the Men's Alliance trademark. Please refer to Appendix 5: Social Media Guide for a full list of rules and recommendations on using social media to promote your tribes. GroupMe Policy GroupMe is the Tribe's main communication channel. Use it well and it builds the Tribe. Use it poorly and it kills it. Purpose of GroupMe GroupMe exists for Tribe business: workout location and weather calls, announcements, prayer requests, devotion heads-ups, and quick coordination during the week. It is not a forum for political debate, theological arguments, meme dumps, or content unrelated to the Tribe. If it wouldn't fly as a devotion topic, it doesn't fly in GroupMe. Don't Remove Inactive Members Leave inactive members in the chat. A man who hasn't shown up in two months may still be reading the messages, still being prayed for, still circling back. Removing him from the group sends the message that he's been dropped. That's the opposite of what we do. The only times you remove a member: he asks to be removed, he's actively disrupting the chat, or he's violating the Honor Code in writing. Otherwise he stays. Tribe Leader Sets the Tone The Tribe Leader sets the tone for the chat. If a thread starts drifting into a fight, shut it down quickly and move the conversation offline. Don't let GroupMe become a place where men come away worse than they arrived. Regional Leader As Backup To backup and support the Tribe Leader, the Regional Leader for that tribe should also be invited and join the tribe GroupMe so he too can help to ensure that the DNA remains consistent and to support the TL and Chiefs. Passing Announcements from Leadership When MA leadership has an announcement that needs to reach every Tribe, here's how it moves: - Leadership pushes the announcement out through the Division Leaders. - Division Leaders forward to their Regional Leaders. - Regional Leaders forward to their Tribe Leaders. - Tribe Leaders deliver the announcement at the next Tribe meeting around the fire, and post it in GroupMe. Don't skip levels. If a Tribe Leader gets an announcement directly from a Division Leader without it coming through his Regional Leader, send it back up the chain and ask the Regional Leader to push it through properly. The chain exists for a reason. Proper Channel for New Ideas Good ideas come from everywhere. Here's how they move so they actually get heard: - Tribesman raises the idea with his Tribe Leader. - Tribe Leader brings it to his Regional Leader. - Regional Leader brings it to his Division Leader. - Division Leader brings it to MA leadership if it's organization-wide. Skipping the chain doesn't make a good idea move faster. It just means it lands on someone's desk without context, and it usually dies there. Run it up the chain the right way. Who to Contact First with Questions or Concerns Tribesman: start with your Tribe Leader. Tribe Leader: start with your Regional Leader. Regional Leader: start with your Division Leader. Division Leader: start with MA leadership. If your immediate leader is the source of the concern, go one level up. Always loop back through the chain when the issue is resolved. When a Man Wants to Start His Own Tribe This is a good problem to have. A current tribesman who has caught the DNA and wants to plant a new Tribe is exactly what we're trying to produce. Here's what to tell him: "Great! Send him to the website to fill out an application. We have an entire team ready to help him successfully launch a new tribe." Attending Multiple Tribes Some men want to attend two or more Tribes per week. That's allowed and often a good thing, especially in cities with multiple Tribes within driving distance. Two things to know: - A man has one home Tribe. That's the Tribe where he was given his callsign and where his roster entry lives. He gets one wingman, one Tribe Leader, one home roster. - Visiting other Tribes is a sign of a healthy ministry. Encourage it. We want men sweating, talking, and praying with brothers from other Tribes. Cross-pollination strengthens the network. - We don't want MA widows. Be careful to avoid overload and burnout and fail at our first mission to lead at home by attending too many tribes. Married tribesmen and those with children need to be especially vigilant about not letting tribe come before family. If a man tries to use a second Tribe to avoid a problem at his home Tribe, that's a Tribe Leader conversation. Otherwise, the door is open. How to Position Men's Alliance to the Church When you sit down with church staff to introduce Men's Alliance, here's the frame that works: Men's Alliance is a discipleship group, led by members at the church for any man who wishes to build stronger bonds of friendship outside while focusing on becoming the men God designed us to be. Men's Alliance was founded and created in Virginia, and is not a competing ministry, we're not a parachurch group trying to pull men away, and we're not a workout program. We exist to take Christian men who already attend that church and sharpen them, then deliver them back to that church as better husbands, fathers, and leaders. The pitch: - MA gets men out of their seats and into a discipleship rhythm: weekly fire, weekly devotion, weekly accountability, expectation to lead and learn scripture. - MA produces men who can lead. We teach leadership through the callsign, coin, and patch progression. By the time a man is patched, he can give the Gospel, defend the faith, and lead a small group of men. - MA stays in its lane. We don't baptize, take communion, sing, or preach church. We're a Tuesday or Thursday morning, not a Sunday. - MA brings men to church. Many of our Tribesmen first showed up at a Tribe before they ever showed up in a pew. The Tribe is the bridge. Bring a copy of this Leadership Guide and the Essentials of Our Faith. Don't oversell. Most pastors say yes when they understand what we actually are.

Men's Alliance is a 501(c)3 nonprofit named Tip of the Spear Ministries. The dictionary defines "tip of the spear" as a group of people that is the first to do something considered difficult or dangerous, the first to enter a battle. Men's Alliance is Tip of the Spear Ministries.

Men's Alliance Tribes that are affiliated with a church fall under the insurance and liability of that church. Any Men's Alliance Tribe not affiliated with a church must have everyone sign our [waiver](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1e72aNdx5BCZTF8UQQv-tr75VntngFJ5q/edit) (located on our website when you create your online account).

The official position of Men's Alliance on alcohol consumption is also the position of Martin Luther, C.S. Lewis, and Jesus. We allow alcohol consumption in moderation. The Bible is very clear that a little wine is good for us (1 Timothy 5:23), and that Jesus and his apostles drank wine in moderation together (Matthew 11:19). Turning water into the best tasting wine at a wedding celebration was in fact the first miracle that Jesus performed (John 2). Furthermore, nowhere in the Bible is alcohol consumption prohibited. However, drinking in excess can lead to the sin of drunkenness (Ephesians 5:18). For this reason, all Men's Alliance Tribesmen will refrain from drinking to excess. We understand that many people struggle with an addiction to alcohol; therefore, as an organization, we will not serve/sell alcohol at any official Men's Alliance event that is open to all Tribesmen. We do not want to be a stumbling block to anyone (Romans 14:21). In Men's Alliance, we do not pretend to be something we are not. Those who drink alcohol do not need to hide it, and those who struggle with alcohol addiction should not hide that either. In Men's Alliance we are the same on Sunday morning as we are on Friday night.

- Don't give yourself pneumonia trying to prove how rugged you are by getting soaked in freezing rain. Get under a pavilion if needed or go indoors if lightning, tornadoes, or other extreme weather events make it foolish to remain outside. Tribe Leaders should cancel anytime they deem the weather unsafe. - Be prepared to lead a workout or devotion or build the fire in case the leader doesn't show up. "In the absence of leadership I will take charge and lead by example." - Consider a Saturday activity of installing pull-up bars, climbing rope, building a fire pit and benches, and constructing a shelter over a woodpile. - If the church asks a Men's Alliance Tribe to help do labor (i.e. mulching, painting, setup, etc) our answer is that we would be happy to help do that, but not during our Tribe meeting. We can easily assemble a work party to help assist the church. But our one-hour Tribe meeting each week needs to be protected. We promise men an epic workout; we cannot deliver anything less. - Do not post any video clips of war cries, or callsign/coin ceremonies. Pictures are fine, but not video. This is a private event and what happens around the fire should stay there. - If a female shows up and wants to participate, you should politely inform her that this group is for biological men only, but do not make a scene. Just carry on with the workout and devotion as usual. Do not give them the YouTube clip they are hoping for. - Men's Alliance welcomes all men to join. Whether you have an addiction, struggle with pornography, or a homosexual lifestyle, we will not turn you away from coming to Men's Alliance and even earning your callsign. However, in order to lead at Men's Alliance (workouts or devotions), a man cannot be using illicit drugs, or living in sexual immorality (that is any sexual activity outside of a God-ordained marriage between one man and one woman). Join us weekly around the fire regardless of where you're at in life. - When a Tribesman moves away and leaves the Tribe, the traditional gift is a sledgehammer signed by all the Tribesmen. When a Tribe Leader hands over a Tribe, the traditional gift is a hatchet with their callsign and Tribe name inscribed on it. We want to take the time to celebrate these men and do something special for them. - Men's Alliance does not partner in or co-host joint events with any woman's ministry. By focusing on events in a men-only setting, we stay true to our mission while honoring and protecting the marriages and families we seek to strengthen. Callsign Change Process In the very rare event that a man wants to change his callsign, he must: - Present his case to the Tribe for a vote as to whether or not he can be renamed. The Tribe will vote, the vote must take place at a Tribe meeting where the Tribe Leader, Workout Chief, and Devotion Chief are all present. - The cost is 30 burpees for the man if the Tribe votes no. This is to prevent frivolous requests. - If it's agreed that he gets a new callsign, then he must recite the motto, verse, and every month's memory verse since he received his first callsign. - If he's able to do that, then he gets a new callsign; however, he does not get a veto this time. Crisis Response If anyone expresses to you that they are thinking about hurting themselves or that their Family would be better off without them, take them seriously. Be a listener. Talk to them and remind them that they do not struggle alone. If you believe that the person might carry out a plan to harm themselves, do not leave that person alone. Stay with them, and then immediately call the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline number 988. (Program it in your phone). Men's Alliance is 100% committed to helping each other out in times of crisis. APPENDIX 1: MEN'S ALLIANCE LEADERSHIP AGREEMENT This agreement must be signed and adhered to by all Men's Alliance Tribe Leaders, Workout and Devotion Chiefs, Region/Division Leaders, Officers, Board Members, and Patched men. Your privilege of using, wearing, and representing Men's Alliance, its logos, and its motto, is contingent upon your adherence to this Agreement, including the Essentials of Our Faith, Honor Code, and the Leadership Guide. Any deviation from these could result in removal from one's leadership position. The Essentials of Our Faith All people have sinned and fallen short of the glory of God. (Romans 3:23) The penalty for sin is death. (Romans 6:23) God, the Creator of the Universe, eternally existing in three persons (Father, Son, and Holy Spirit), came to earth as the person of Jesus. Jesus is God in human form. (Colossians 1:15) Jesus was born of a virgin, lived a sinless life, and willingly died on a Roman cross to pay the death penalty for our sins. He rose from the dead on the third day just as He predicted. (1 Corinthians 15:3-8) Salvation is a free gift that is available to everyone. We are saved by grace and not by our good works or any human efforts. (Ephesians 2:8-9) "If we confess with our mouth that Jesus is Lord, and believe in our hearts that God raised Him from the dead we will be saved." (Romans 10:9) Jesus made the powerful claim to be the only way to God the Father. This means that Jesus is either the only way or He is a liar, but having made such a claim, He logically cannot be just a good teacher. (John 14:6) The Bible is our blueprint for how we should live and what we believe. When faced with conflicting ideas from culture or personal feelings, we trust Scripture to guide us in truth. (Psalm 119:105) The Men's Alliance Leadership Honor Code As an essential part of the Men's Alliance Leadership Team, you have a responsibility to develop and exhibit mature Christian behavior. This should be the basic premise of your desire to participate in a servant-leader position in Men's Alliance. While serving the Body of Christ as a servant-leader in Men's Alliance, you should strive to demonstrate Biblical standards in all situations. As Christians, the way we present ourselves to others is of vital importance to the way others perceive Christ. Our conduct should never be an embarrassment to Christ, but should exemplify the best qualities of a mature believer and servant-leader. Exemplifying the highest moral commitment, Men's Alliance leaders are to maintain a disciplined life. We should engage in daily Bible reading and prayer as the foundation upon which everything else in our lives is built upon. Furthermore, as a leader of MA Tribesmen, you are expected to continuously work on sharpening yourself physically as well as spiritually. This would include physical exercises outside of MA workouts, to include lifting weights, rucking, running, biking, swimming, etc., to the extent that God has physically enabled you. A Men's Alliance leader should be both warrior and disciple, barbarian and ambassador. We should regard it as an essential part of our Tribal culture, not as an imposition or restriction. APPENDIX 5: SOCIAL MEDIA GUIDE Overview Every post from a Men's Alliance tribe page is a public representation of this ministry and this brotherhood. The standard is simple: Tribal. Rugged. Real. What you post should look like who we are. Account Administration All Men's Alliance social media accounts must be created and administered by HQ. Tribe Leaders may be granted editor rights to manage content. HQ reserves the right to add or remove admins or editors, or revoke access, at any time. Account naming must follow this format: Men's Alliance - [Tribe Name] (e.g., Men's Alliance - Alpha Tribe). Do not use names that imply independence from Men's Alliance (e.g., "Shady Grove Men's Ministry"). The pre-approved Tribe-specific logo must be used as the profile photo on all tribe accounts. All account descriptions must identify Men's Alliance as the trade name for Tip of the Spear Ministries, include the mission statement, and link to mensalliancetribe.com. What to Post Default to photos of the tribe together. We are out of isolation. A photo of the brothers is almost always the right choice. The best content shows men in it together: praying with arms around each other at the fire, grinding through a workout, struggling side by side. Three to seven men in frame. That is the tribe. That is what this looks like. Authentic beats polished every time. A grainy photo of men working out in the rain tells the truth. A staged, overproduced image does not. Snap the moment. Do not over-edit it. What Not to Post Do not post photos of one man posing alone. That is not tribal and it is not what this ministry is about. Do not post video of men on the tire. The tire is a sacred tribe moment. What happens on the tire stays on the tire. Do not use AI-generated images. We want real men, not a computer's idea of what men look like. The authenticity of this brotherhood is the brand. AI imagery is prohibited. Do not post anything political, partisan, or culture-war in nature. No humor at the expense of men, fathers, churches, or other religions. Content must be family-friendly and suitable for representing Christ publicly. Do not use imagery that looks military or militia: no camouflage uniforms, tactical rifles, combat gear, or group photos posed like a unit. Many of us are veterans. Men's Alliance is not a military organization and must not be presented as one. Logo Use on Social Media Only officially approved Men's Alliance logos may appear in posts, graphics, and event promotions. Use logos exactly as provided. Do not stretch, compress, rotate, recolor, or modify any logo in any way. Do not place logos on busy or clashing backgrounds that reduce legibility. Tribes may not add their tribe name to an official MA logo. Each tribe has its own pre-approved tribe-specific logo. Use that. When promoting events, posts must include official MA branding: approved logo, approved colors, approved fonts. Logo Rules ,  Non-Negotiable Logos are locked. No alterations, distortions, stretching, rotating, overlays, recoloring, or creative adjustments of any kind are permitted. Specifically: - Do not stretch, compress, or rotate any logo - Do not change logo colors or add effects (shadows, gradients, filters) - Do not place a logo on a busy or clashing background - Do not add elements, taglines, or tribe names to an official MA logo - Do not use low-resolution, pixelated, or outdated versions of any logo - Do not crop or cut off any part of a logo Tribes may not add their own names to the official MA logos. Each tribe receives a separate, pre-approved tribe-specific logo created by or approved by the ministry. Content Tone Men's Alliance speaks with a voice that is masculine, direct, and bold; Christ-centered and grounded in Scripture; encouraging but never soft; and brotherly, calling men in rather than calling men out. Keep posts consistent with that voice. No corporate language. No watered-down messaging. No crude or vulgar content. Photography Rights All photos and videos taken at Men's Alliance events are the property of Tip of the Spear Ministries. Tribes may use their own event photos for local promotion, but by capturing or submitting media, they grant Men's Alliance non-exclusive, perpetual, royalty-free rights to use, edit, and distribute that media for ministry purposes. Questions Direct all branding questions to [brand@mensalliancetribe.com](mailto:brand@mensalliancetribe.com). APPENDIX 6: FINANCE FAQ The questions below come up over and over. This appendix is the official MA position. If a donor, Tribe Leader, or pastor asks about money, point them here. Q: Are donations to my local Tribe tax-deductible? A: No. Donations made directly to a local Tribe, whether handed to the Tribe Leader, dropped in a hat at a fundraiser, or sent Venmo to a Tribesman, are not tax-deductible. They are personal gifts to the Tribe and the Tribe handles them locally. Q: Are donations to Tip of the Spear Ministries tax-deductible? A: Yes. Tip of the Spear Ministries is a registered 501(c)(3) nonprofit. Donations made to Tip of the Spear through the official MA giving channels (the website donate page or by check to the registered address) are tax-deductible to the full extent allowed by law. Q: If a man wants a tax-deductible receipt, what should I tell him? A: Direct him to www.mensalliancetribe.com to donate to Tip of the Spear Ministries through the official channel. He'll get a receipt that he can use for his taxes. Local Tribe donations don't generate that receipt. Q: Can a local Tribe raise money for its own needs? A: Yes. Local Tribes can take up donations for firewood, equipment, community service projects, gifts for departing Tribesmen, and other Tribe-level expenses. The Tribe Leader and the Finance Chief (if designated) keep simple records. These funds stay at the Tribe level and are not run through Tip of the Spear. Q: Who handles local Tribe fundraising? A: The Finance Chief, if the Tribe has one. If not, the Tribe Leader. The job is to track money in, money out, and what each dollar was raised for. Keep it simple, keep it honest, and report to the Tribe periodically so everyone knows where the money went. Q: Can a Tribe run a fundraiser for a Tribesman in crisis? A: Yes. If a Tribesman or his Family is going through a real crisis (medical, job loss, funeral expenses), the Tribe can take up a love offering or run a fundraiser. The Finance Chief or Tribe Leader collects, the money goes directly to the man in need, and the Tribe Leader documents what was raised and what was paid out. Q: What is the Annual Birthday Party and how does it fundraise? A: The MA Birthday Party is an annual gathering where multiple Tribes come together for fellowship and a kickoff to the year. It is the main fundraising event for Tip of the Spear Ministries. Tickets and sponsorships go to Tip of the Spear and are tax-deductible. Tribes are encouraged to fill tables, sponsor a table, donate craft items to an auction, participate in raffles, or invite supporters who might back the ministry. Q: Can my Tribe keep some of what it raises at the MA Annual Birthday Party? A: No. The Birthday Party raises money for Tip of the Spear Ministries, not for individual Tribes. If your Tribe needs local funding, run a separate local fundraiser. Don't mix the two. Q: Can a Tribe accept money from a corporation or a non-Christian donor? A: Yes, with one rule: no donor controls the message. If a corporate or outside donor wants to give to support Men's Alliance, the donation is welcome. But they don't get a vote on what we teach, who leads, or what we say from the fire. If a donor wants control, refuse the gift. Q: Can a Tribesman be reimbursed for things he buys for the Tribe? A: Yes, if the Tribe Leader approves it in advance. Don't make purchases for the Tribe and expect reimbursement after the fact unless you've cleared it. Keep receipts. The Finance Chief or Tribe Leader handles the reimbursement out of local Tribe funds. Q: Who do I contact with a finance question that's not covered here? A: Start with your Tribe Leader. If he doesn't know, he goes to his Regional Leader. If that doesn't resolve it, the question goes up to MA leadership at info@mensalliancetribe.com APPENDIX 7: SENSITIVE SITUATIONS Some situations come up that don't have a clean playbook. This appendix gives you the MA position on a few of them. When in doubt, run it up the chain. A Tribesman Discloses Abuse If a Tribesman tells you he was abused as a child or that he is currently in an abusive situation, take it seriously. Listen. Don't try to fix it on the spot. Tell him you're glad he trusted you with it, and ask him what kind of support he needs. Encourage him to talk to a licensed counselor and his pastor. Offer to help him find one if he doesn't have one. Don't try to counsel him yourself unless that's your professional training. If the disclosure involves ongoing harm to a minor, you are required to report it to local authorities. This is a legal obligation in most jurisdictions, not a judgment call. Refer to the [MA Sexual Abuse Prevention Policy](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1TQAmjNjqTKc8lPjOjJE4HhaJWUjbuqNFKagljBkkDRg/edit?usp=sharing) for full reporting steps. A Tribesman Discloses an Affair or Active Sin If a man around the fire confesses an affair, addiction, or ongoing serious sin, the Tribe is the right place for that confession. Don't shame him. Encourage him to confess to his wife (if applicable) and his pastor, and to get into counseling. If he's in a formal leadership role at MA (Tribe Leader, Chief, Regional Leader, Division Leader, Board, Patched), the Honor Code applies. He needs to step down from leadership while he works through repentance and restoration. This is not punishment; it's the gate to real healing. The Regional Leader and MA leadership work with him on a path back to leadership when he's ready. A Tribesman in Mental Health Crisis Suicide threats, talk of self-harm, or talk of harming others, take seriously every time. Do not leave the man alone. Call 988 (National Suicide Prevention Lifeline). If he is in immediate danger, call 911. After the immediate crisis: connect him with his pastor, a licensed counselor, and his wingman. Loop in the Tribe Leader and the Regional Leader. Keep the circle of people who know small, but make sure the man has support. A Visitor Is Disruptive, Hostile, or Unsafe If a visitor shows up under the influence, becomes hostile to other Tribesmen, or behaves in a way that's clearly unsafe, the Tribe Leader has the authority to ask him to leave. Do it calmly. Don't escalate. If he refuses, end the workout or move the Tribe to a different location. Don't fight him on the spot. After the event, document what happened and brief the Regional Leader. Conflict Between Tribesmen Conflict happens. Apply Matthew 18: the offended Tribesman goes to the other one privately first. If that doesn't resolve the issue, bring in a third party (the Tribe Leader). If it still doesn't resolve the issue, the Tribe Leader brings in the Regional Leader. Don't let a conflict simmer. Don't let it spread through GroupMe or side conversations. Get it on the table and work it out. APPENDIX 8: LEADERSHIP HANDOFF Every Tribe Leader will hand off the Tribe eventually. The handoff is a feature of MA, not a problem. We train our replacements, and the Tribe outlives any one leader. When to Hand Off Tribe Leaders are encouraged to hand off about every 18 months. Chiefs about every 12 months. These aren't hard deadlines, they're defaults. Hand off when: - You've hit the 18-month mark and you have a man ready to take it. - Your life season is changing (new job, new baby, family situation) and you can't give the Tribe what it needs. - You're burned out. Don't white-knuckle it. Hand off. - A clearly more gifted leader has emerged in the Tribe and the Tribe is hungry for him. Who Takes Over The next Tribe Leader must be a coined member, in good standing, who has signed the Leadership Agreement and agrees to the Essentials of Our Faith. He needs the current Tribe Leader's recommendation and the Regional Leader's approval. Don't hand off to the loudest guy. Hand off to the most trusted guy. There's a difference. The Handoff Process - Current Tribe Leader identifies the candidate and runs the name by the Regional Leader. - Regional Leader approves or pushes back. - Current Tribe Leader has the conversation with the candidate. Confirms he's willing. - Candidate shadows the current Tribe Leader for at least 4 weeks: opening the workout, leading announcements, handling the GroupMe, running the callsign and coin process. - Public handoff at a Tribe gathering. Current Tribe Leader presents the hatchet (callsign and Tribe name inscribed). New Tribe Leader is prayed over by the Tribe. - Former Tribe Leader stays in the Tribe for at least 3 months after handoff, attending and supporting, but not leading. This keeps continuity and gives the new leader cover to make changes. After Handoff The former Tribe Leader does not interfere with the new Tribe Leader's decisions. Different leaders run things differently. If the former Tribe Leader sees something going sideways, he talks to the new Tribe Leader privately, or if needed, to the Regional Leader. Never undermine the new leader in front of the Tribe. The former Tribe Leader is still a valuable resource. The new Tribe Leader is encouraged to ask him questions and use his experience. But the decisions are the new leader's. FOREWORD "Get out of your comfort zone!" These words have been uttered many times by Dave "Goose" Mills in that all-familiar North Carolina accent. Getting out of your comfort zone is the only way to grow and learn. I met Goose in the fall of 2020. What a crazy time for the world. In the midst of it all, I was introduced to Goose and a very simple ministry designed to bring men out of isolation, out of their comfort zones, and out from behind those façades that some of us had spent our entire lives creating. These weekly gatherings, which included an outdoor thirty-minute workout and a thirty-minute devotion around a fire, were only the beginning of me being pushed outside my own comfort zone. Goose had discovered a brilliant yet simple formula that connected men and formed a bond with one another in a way that I had not experienced since my days in the military. ix

Since that time, I have seen so many men benefit from belonging to a tribe. Many of them are now some of my closest friends and I will consider brothers. Swinging sledgehammers, flipping tires, and doing countless burpees before we gathered around a fire to share our real-world struggles as men were only the beginning of my discomfort. There was a quick realization that Goose had more in mind. Sharpening myself physically and spiritually by meeting with my tribe weekly was one thing, but now he was talking about becoming a barbarian ambassador. What was that? Little did I know that I was about to become the beneficiary of the years of strategic leadership training and experience that Goose acquired over his twenty years in the United States Air Force. This book is an important piece of that leadership, discipleship, and apologetics training. In Romans, Paul writes, "Do not conform to the pattern of this world but be transformed by the renewing of your mind" (Rom 12:2). This is at the heart of what it means to be a barbarian, someone who refuses to conform to the prevailing culture. Will your x

neighbors be surprised to find out that you are a follower of Jesus? It's easy to fall into the trap of looking and acting like those around us. But we are called to be different, to be set apart, to be barbarians! Paul again writes in his second letter to the church in Corinth that we are ambassadors for Christ. "We are therefore Christ's ambassadors, as though God were making his appeal through us. We implore you on Christ's behalf: Be reconciled to God" (2 Cor 5:20). Sometimes, I wonder why he has chosen to reveal himself to the world through people like you and me. I often feel like Moses, unworthy of speaking on behalf of God (Ex 4:10). Maybe you feel the same way. This book will change that. There are hundreds of books on Christian apologetics that you can choose to read. This one boils it down and presents it in an easy-to-understand format for the common man. This book will provide you with the tools needed to defend and share your faith with more confidence than ever before. Goose has written it to help you on your journey to become the barbarian ambassador that God has designed you to be! Ray "Thor" Wageman xi

Lewis, William Lane Craig, Frank Turek, Greg Koukl, John Lennox, Norman Geisler, Nabeel Qureshi, Lee Strobel, Hugh Ross, Gary Habermas, J. Warner Wallace, Alvin Plantinga, J. P. Moreland, Dan Wallace, James White, Andy Stanley, and Tim Barnett. I sincerely thank these men for the work they've spent their lives doing to share their wisdom and tactics. Reading and listening to them have changed the way I represent Jesus to those around me. Additionally, I wouldn't have any of this knowledge without Aaron Boykin and Mike Flynn putting together and leading the weekly apologetics group I attend. Aaron's knowledge on Christian apologetics is extraordinary, and I consider it a blessing to learn from him. xiii
Last, writing this book has been my wife Kerry's idea. And for her to think I should write a manual is no small thing since normally she tells me that I need to do less. xiv

INTRODUCTION You're driving your son and his friend home one evening, and you ask the friend if he goes to church anywhere. He replies, "No, sir, I'm an atheist." Your son looks at you and raises his eyebrows. The friend continues. "There's just no way anyone can know if there's actually an afterlife or not, and I don't believe all the stories in the Bible anyway." You're at lunch with your buddy and his father, and the television in the restaurant shows a commercial for the Bible on DVD. Your friend's dad says, "There are so many errors and contradictions in the Bible, I don't know how anybody can actually believe it." You're sharing a rental car with a coworker when they ask, "You're a Christian, right?" You say yes, wondering where this is about to go. xv

They reply, "I just can't be a Christian because a loving God would want to stop all the evil in the world, and a powerful God could stop the evil. So, if there's any god at all, he's clearly not all loving and all powerful." Will these moments catch you off guard? Will you be flustered with a half-baked reply? Will you even speak up at all? The problem is 70 percent of Christian teenagers claim they are no longer Christians after their first year of college.1 This shows that fathers are probably spending more time playing fun games and eating pizza with their kids than teaching them what they believe and why. My goal is to help you teach your kids that Christianity doesn't require you to check your intellect at the door. Jesus commands us to love God with all our mind. So, we're not being fully loving if we don't engage our intellect in our faith. If we are to become spiritual leaders in our home, then we must be so confident and so prepared that we look forward to the tough questions. Far from catching us off guard and flustered, these moments are a gift from God! xvi

I know what you're thinking. You think that you aren't qualified to lead anyone spiritually. You know all your sins and failures, and you feel you aren't good enough to lead your family. You're afraid they'll see right through you and call you a hypocrite. Here are three tips to help you lead your family spiritually: Tip 1: You're not perfect. Lead anyway. Paul wrote in the book of Romans that every person has sinned and fallen short of the glory of God. Satan wants to remind you of your sins, throw them back in your face, and get you to stay quiet on the sidelines. Tip 2: Admit your faults. Tell your family that you know you're not perfect by a long stretch but that you want to help them fully understand why Jesus died on a cross and how that changes literally everything. Tip 3: Read the answer key. If you already know the questions they're going to ask you, and you are prepared with the answers beforehand, then you'll be much more confident and willing to lead others spiritually. This manual is designed to give you those answers and the confidence that goes with them. xvii

News flash! We are ambassadors! According to 2 Corinthians 5:20, we are commanded to be ambassadors for Jesus. And our family is our first place of assignment. If you were the American ambassador to China, you would certainly prepare by learning the answers to the questions you would likely be asked, right? You'd brush up on the GNP, trade laws, and regional military forces, and you'd be prepared to answer questions like What makes America different from other nations? I'm quite sure that God had that preparation in mind when he chose to call us ambassadors. What kind of ambassador for Jesus will we be if we don't know what makes us different from a Mormon or a Muslim? What kind of ambassador will we be if we are caught off guard by the most common questions that people have been asking for years? Ambassadors for Jesus Christ should know both the questions and the answers to accurately represent their King. My Personal Story I spent far too many years not speaking out for Jesus because I was afraid of appearing stupid or xviii

hypocritical if I opened my mouth. When we don't know what questions will be asked, and we don't have any answers prepared, we make terrible ambassadors. It was not until I joined an apologetics group several years ago that my mind began growing in knowledge of how to answer the tough questions that I'd been avoiding my whole life. I learned that all questions (and accusations) fit into only a few different categories (or buckets) and that each bucket was fi lled with illogical arguments propped up by rhetoric. My confidence in my own faith began to grow as I learned that Christianity actually does have the answers. The answers are all there! They are logical, consistent, and coherent. It's just that most Christians are such bad ambassadors that the world doesn't know this. Imagine if all the US ambassadors to China for the last fifty years had been high school dropouts. We wouldn't be surprised if China didn't think very highly of America. The reason many Americans today are turning away from Christianity has nothing to do with the message of Jesus. It has everything to do with his ambassadors! xix

The word apologetics comes from the Greek work apologia, meaning a well-reasoned argument. Some Christians think apologetics is a separate discipline that is only appealing to certain people. That view is flat wrong. If you are a Christian, then you'd better be an apologist. The apostle Peter calls us all out in 1 Peter 3:15: "Always be prepared to give an answer to everyone who asks you to give the reason for the hope that you have. But do this with gentleness and respect." We simply cannot be bold ambassadors for Jesus without being "prepared to give an answer." My goal in writing this is to help prepare you to give an answer and become a better ambassador for Jesus of Nazareth. May this be a resource that helps you boldly and accurately lead your family spiritually. David "Goose" Mills xx

THE PURPOSE OF APOLOGETICS As Alister McGrath says in Mere Apologetics, Apologetics the goal of apologetics is not to win arguments or argue someone into Christianity. Apologetics is about removing the obstacles that are in people's way. Sometimes, those obstacles have to do with science, suffering, miracles, or history. Once their path is cleared of obstacles, it is then that evangelism invites the person to walk down the cleared path.2 Apologetics and evangelism work hand in hand. In Acts 2, Peter used an apologetic argument as he spoke to fellow Jews. He tailored his language according to his audience by citing Old Testament (OT) predictions of a Messiah and referring to Abraham, Isaac, 1

ANSWER WITH TRUTH and Jacob. These words and themes would not be persuasive to anyone other than a Jewish audience. So, Peter was not using a one-size-fits-all message about Jesus, but rather he used an argument that would be effective with that specific audience. Likewise, as Paul spoke in Acts 17 to a Greek audience, he did not use any of those Old Testament terms because they would place more obstacles rather than remove them. Instead, Paul quoted a Greek poet and identified Jesus Christ as the "unknown God" to whom the Greeks had an altar. This was truly speaking the language of his audience!3 Just as Peter and Paul tailored their words so that they would be clearly received by their audience, so, too, should we learn to speak the language of our audience. We must meet people where they are, use words they understand and arguments they will find persuasive, and remove the obstacles that they personally struggle with. This requires us to do a lot of listening before we speak. For example, if a person believes the Bible is a fourth-century invention written to control the masses, then it will be futile to say to them that the Bible says such and such. You must first remove 2

THE PURPOSE OF APOLOGETICS the obstacle between them and the reliability of New Testament (NT) manuscripts. We will fail as ambassadors if we do not first get to know our audience, ask them what they believe, ask them if they've considered Christianity, and if they've rejected it, ask them why. And just as Paul used arguments familiar to the Greeks in Athens, so we must use arguments that resonate today. 3

LEADING AT HOME The most important battle that we'll ever fight is for the souls of our children. Yet, somehow, we spend more time taking them to soccer practice and talking about their grades in school than discussing with them what we believe and why. I think this neglect comes from not knowing what to say exactly and a fear of their responding with a question (or accusation) that makes us look foolish. News flash! There are only three main categories. Most objections to Christianity that your kids will have can fit into one of three buckets. The way questions are worded may change over the years, but the three major buckets don't. If you learn how to address each bucket and to recognize in which bucket your 4

LEADING AT HOME kid's objections go, then (like 1 Peter 3:15 commands) you'll always be prepared to give an answer. For each objection to Christianity, I will give you both a short answer, which is what you'll want to use at the dinner table or around the watercooler, and then a deeper answer for if that person is genuinely seeking truth and wants to follow up with you later. THE THREE BIG BUCKETS Bucket 1: Relativism Relativism says there is no truth. You can't really know anything for certain. You have your truth, and I have mine. Whatever works for you is fine, and whatever works for someone else is acceptable too. It's all good. Don't try to push your truth on others. Everything is relative. The relativism bucket is fast growing in America. It's a crowd-pleaser, and using it makes you sound like the cool, laid-back peacemaker. It works great at parties, and for that reason, your kids might be attracted to it at some point. 5

ANSWER WITH TRUTH Short answer: Is it true that there is no truth? Do you know for certain that you can't know anything for certain? Is relativism true for all people everywhere? You see, the problem with relativism is that it is logically self-defeating. As soon as you apply the claim of relativism to itself, it cuts off the very limb that it's sitting on. You might also ask them if it's true that George Washington was the first president of the United States. So, there are historical truths truths. Is the Nile River in Egypt? So, there are geographic truths. truths Does two plus two equal four? So, there are mathematical truths.. Do two hydrogen molecules and one oxygen molecule make water? So, there are scientific truths.. So, I guess not everything is relative, right? And, since we've all just agreed that there are historic, geographic, mathematic, and scientific truths, isn't it possible that there are also moral and religious truths? Deeper answer: Is racism, discrimination, and child sex trafficking objectively wrong for all people everywhere? If they say yes, then they are admitting that relativism is not true by confessing that universal objective 6

LEADING AT HOME morality does exist (more on that as evidence for the existence of God in appendix 1). If they say no, then they are indeed being consistent, but they are admitting that all heinous injustices (such as genocide and torture) are merely matters of personal opinion. And if those are just matters of opinion (like your favorite ice cream), then they shouldn't try to stop others from doing them. Ouch! Bottom line: If your worldview cannot condemn genocide, torture, and racism (and it's logically self-defeating), then you seriously need to get yourself a new worldview. If your daughter says she's become a relativist, it is highly likely that she's never really considered it this far. She's probably just repeating a bumper sticker philosophy picked up during her freshman year of college, and she's never been challenged on it. (This makes them bad ambassadors for relativism.) So, tread lightly. Don't try to rub their face in it. Just lovingly explain the logical problem as well as the deeper implications of the relativistic worldview. 7

Bucket 2: Pluralism Pluralism says that all paths lead to the same place. We all worship the same God; we just know him by different names. It's arrogant to think that only your path is the correct one. Religions are like blind men feeling an elephant. One thinks it's like a rope, the other a tree trunk, another a wall, but they're all describing different parts of the same thing. Pluralism is the ultimate crowd-pleaser. It's so warm and fuzzy, progressive, and universal. Golly gee, I sure do hope we're all still pals. Pluralism makes me think of Matthew 7:13, "Enter through the narrow gate; for the gate is wide and the way is broad that leads to destruction, and there are many who enter through it. For the gate is small and the way is narrow that leads to life, and there are few who find it." Short answer: What did Jesus have to say about other paths? (If you're holding a mic, then you can drop it at this point.) John 14:6 tells us that Jesus said, "I am the way, the truth, and the life, and no one comes to the Father except through me." So, as C. S. Lewis famously said, either Jesus is the only way or he's a liar.4 But he clearly isn't one of many good ways.

LEADING AT HOME Deeper answer: Pluralism violates the law of logic known as the law of noncontradiction, which states that something cannot be both true and false at the same time. For example, the light in the room can be on, or the light can be off, but, logically, it cannot be both. While it is logically possible that all religions are false or that only one religion is true, they cannot all be true since one of them (Christianity) makes a claim to be the only way. Christianity is exclusive. It excludes all other gods, paths, good works, idols, and lucky charms. First Timothy 2:5 says, "For there is one God and one mediator between God and mankind, the man Christ Jesus." And Acts 4:12 says, "Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved." Bottom line: If your worldview contradicts logic and calls Jesus a liar, then you seriously need to get yourself a new worldview. So, if your son comes home from college saying things in this pluralism bucket, just lovingly remind him that Jesus made an exclusive truth claim, so he's either the only way or he's a liar. 9

ANSWER WITH TRUTH Bucket 3: Scientism Also called materialism or naturalism, scientism states that only science gives us truth. Everything else, such as religion, philosophy, and morals, is just opinions. Molecules and atoms are all that exist in this universe, and if it isn't made of those, then it's just your opinion. If relativism is the stoner at the party that questions whether consciousness really exists, and pluralism is the guy who just wants everyone to get along, then scientism is wearing a tweed jacket and smoking a pipe. Scientism is condescending to all other academic disciplines by claiming to have exclusive access to knowledge and truth. (And they think Christians are arrogant!) Short answer: The claim of scientism is also logically self-defeating. Can you prove through a scientific experiment that only science gives us truth? Or is that very statement just a personal opinion about science? Ouch. Scientism itself is not even science but rather a philosophical theory about science.5 The person making the statement has no testable, scientific evidence that scientism is true. They can't prove that the 10

LEADING AT HOME supernatural does not exist. That's outside the realm of science. Deeper answer: Do you believe that justice, love, logic, and mathematics exist? Are they made of molecules and atoms? What about your love for your wife, mom, and so forth? Is that real? Can its atoms be weighed or measured? In fact, there are many moral truths that we know with greater certainty than scientific truths. For example, our definition of planet keeps changing, our knowledge of electrons has changed many times in the last seventy years, and as we learn new things, it changes what we thought we knew.6 There's an entire Wikipedia page on superseded theories. But there is nothing that we will learn in the future to change our moral laws that rape, genocide, kidnapping, and so forth are wrong. Those truths cannot be tested through science, yet we know them with greater certainty. Why is that? Where have these moral laws come from? (At this point, if they begin to claim it is a social construct, refer back to relativism.) Some materialists will claim that all your thoughts, feelings, and emotions are merely the products of 11

ANSWER WITH TRUTH chemical reactions in your brain. Then ask them if a person who sexually molests a child is responsible for their crime. Or are they just "dancing to their DNA" as Richard Dawkins suggests? If your son or daughter claims that all thoughts are merely the product of chemical reactions in their brain, then ask them why they trust their thoughts to come to that very conclusion.7 Bottom line: If your worldview claims to be 100 percent scientific, yet it cannot be scientifically proved itself, then you need to find yourself a new worldview. And if it claims that pedophiles are just dancing to their DNA, then you need to run from it! 12

WHAT DOES A CHRISTIAN BELIEVE? Now that we've shot holes in the three buckets of relativism, pluralism, and scientism, let's talk about the correct worldview. Let's define what exactly Christianity is. While it's easy to get lost in the weeds of the age of the earth, the methods of baptism, or the five points of Calvinism, those things have nothing to do with becoming a Christian. Christians debate all kinds of minor details. So, let's make sure we're clear on the major point. Here's our statement of faith that really hits what's important. 13

ANSWER WITH TRUTH THE MEN'S ALLIANCE STATEMENT OF FAITH The Holy Bible, and only the Bible, is the authoritative Word of God. There is one God, eternally existent in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. These three are coequal and coeternal. Jesus Christ is God the Son, the second person of the Trinity. On the earth, Jesus was 100 percent God and 100 percent man. He is the only man ever to have lived a sinless life. He was born of a virgin, performed miracles, died on the cross for mankind, and thus atoned for our sins through the shedding of his blood. He rose from the dead on the third day according to the scriptures, ascended to the right hand of the Father, and will return again in power and glory. We are saved by grace through faith in Jesus Christ: his death, burial, and resurrection. Salvation is a gift from God, not a result of our good works or of any human efforts. 14

WHAT DOES A CHRISTIAN BELIEVE? WHAT MAKES CHRISTIANITY UNIQUE? A lot of high school and college students are under the impression that all religions are basically the same. This is because they are completely ignorant about what different religions teach. While it's true that there are a few very superficial similarities between religions (like they all have a version of the Golden Rule), their differences are far more important than their similarities. A glass of water may be 99 percent similar to a glass of water with one drop of cyanide in it, but believe me, that 1 percent difference is a lifeand-death issue. So here are some key points to illustrate the absolute uniqueness of Christianity. 1. Christianity is the only non-works-based religion in the world. Mormons, Jews, Buddhists, Hindus, and Muslims are all trying to keep laws, commands, rules, teachings, and pillars to earn their way to paradise, heaven, or nirvana. 2. Christianity alone teaches that you can never ever be good enough to get yourself to heaven. 15

ANSWER WITH TRUTH That's why our Savior had to come down to earth and rescue us. Ephesians 2:8-9 says, "For it is by grace you have been saved through faith, and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God, not by works, so that no one can boast." Believe. That's it (see Acts 16:31). 3. Christianity is based on a single historical event that occurred in the spring of AD 33,  the resurrection of Jesus after being crucified by the Romans. This event was verified by over five hundred witnesses over a forty-day period (1 Cor 15). These witnesses wrote about it, translated it, circulated it all around the world, made no money, died painfully, never recanted, and started the largest movement in all human history over their claim to having seen a man rise from the dead. Notice how the teachings of Jesus did not change their lives. They denied him and ran away after hearing his teachings. But it was the resurrection that changed their lives. No other religion claims anything similar. 4. It's the only religion that has a coherent, consistent answer to all of life's biggest questions. 16

WHAT DOES A CHRISTIAN BELIEVE? Why are we here? Where have we come from? Where are we going? Why is there suffering and pain? Why do bad things happen to good people? Who is Jesus? Why is his tomb empty? Some worldviews answer a couple of these questions. Others answer only one. Some religions deny the question even exists. (For example, Hinduism teaches there is no suffering and evil; it's all an illusion.8 Tell that to a woman who's just been raped. If your worldview says there is no evil, then you seriously need a new worldview.) 5. It is the only religion not based on a book or anyone's teachings. Without the Book of Mormon, you have no Mormons. Without the Koran, you have no Muslims. Without the Torah, you have no one practicing Judaism. Without the Vedas, there will be no Hindus. And without the teachings of Buddha, there will not be Buddhists. But if there had never been a Bible, there would still be Christians. Because … 17

THE BIBLE Here's a question you may have never asked yourself: Do you have to believe the Bible to be a Christian? Think about this: were there Christians before there was a Bible? Obviously. Early Christians had no Bible. Can a person be a Christian if they're illiterate? What if they have no Bible in their language or have only a New Testament? We need to be crystal clear on the only requirement for becoming a Christian, and that is believing in the atoning death and resurrection of Jesus. So, we can conclude that reading the Bible is not a prerequisite for becoming a Christian. What about the Old Testament, the Torah as the Jews call it? Does your daughter have to believe that every story in it is literal? Is believing that Lot's wife turned into an actual pillar of salt a requirement for becoming a Christian? What if your son thinks the six-day creation story in Genesis is meant to be poetic and figurative, not literal? Does that mean they're not Christians? Of course not! Did Paul tell the Roman guard in Acts chapter 16 that he had to believe the stories of Noah and the ark and Jonah and the fish? No. He simply told him, "Believe on the Lord Jesus Christ and you will be saved." 18

WHAT DOES A CHRISTIAN BELIEVE? Remember this: Christianity is not like a Jenga tower where if you can pull out one piece, it will all collapse.9 Christianity has its foundation in just one thing, the resurrection of Jesus. So, if anyone wants to disprove Christianity, they must disprove the resurrection. And people have been trying to do that for nearly two thousand years without success. I ask these questions about the Bible because most American Christians haven't thought them through. But it is paramount to know, if we're going to be good ambassadors, exactly what it is that makes a person a Christian. Christianity is based on one belief: that Jesus died and rose again to pay for your sins sins. Period. Romans 10:9 says, "If you declare with your mouth, 'Jesus is LORD,' and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved." John 3:16 reiterates that "whoever believes in Him will be saved." Believe that Jesus Christ has died and risen again to pay for your sins. That's it. There is no laundry list. 19

ANSWER WITH TRUTH The reason I'm telling you this about the Bible is so that you'll be a better ambassador to your kid when they come home from school saying they aren't a Christian anymore because they no longer believe in the parting of the Red Sea or in Adam and Eve. I want to equip you for that conversation with tactic 1.

Tactic 1, Don't Take the OT Bait Don't get bogged down in the Torah. If your son or daughter says they aren't a Christian because of Noah and the ark or Jonah and the fish and so forth, just simply inform them that those stories are not essential for Christianity. You're a Christian because of the resurrection of Jesus Christ Christ,, not because you believe that Joshua commanded the sun to stand still. This is a tactical maneuver to sidestep a roadblock and continue on course. If you spent an hour explaining archaeological evidence for a global flood and detailing how, based on the dimensions of the ark (300 × 50 × 30 cubits), all the animals could fit in the ark (which could hold 450 shipping containers) with room to spare, they will just bring up a different issue. Don't get caught in the weeds of trying to prove all the miracles of the Old Testament. Sidestep them. They are not the real reason for someone not being a Christian. 20

WHAT DOES A CHRISTIAN BELIEVE? A good question to ask a family member who brings up Noah or Jonah is this: if hypothetically it could be proved that this event happened, would you worship Jesus? I believe you will find that everyone says no. They will just bring up something else they have a problem with. A good approach is to say, "I doubt that you're turning away from Jesus because of stories found in the Torah. Why don't you tell me what's really keeping you from Christianity?" News flash! Romans chapter 1 tells us that what can be known about God is clear to everyone. All people see creation and know in their hearts that there is a God. But some people choose to suppress that truth so that they can continue sinning and pretend they are not accountable to a creator. Let's face it, most college students don't want to follow Jesus because they want to have sex outside of marriage with whomever they want. But since they're not going to admit that, they need to make up something that sounds better, something from one of the buckets. That's what is really keeping people from Christianity, not Balam's talking donkey (Nm 22). 21

ANSWER WITH TRUTH When I am sharing my faith with friends or family, I will allow them the possibility that whatever Old Testament story they bring up may be metaphorical or figurative. I'll concede that the six-day creation account might be using poetic language, not literal.10 (Read Seven Days That Divide the World by Lennox if you want to go deeper on this topic.) I'll allow that I don't know if some of the stories are meant to be literal or not. Maybe. I don't know. They're not really vital to Christianity. I'm not an expert on the Torah; I'm not Jewish. I'm a Christian because of the resurrection of Jesus. Keep bringing it back to the main point. (Later, I will give you another tactic for handling OT story questions more directly.) 22

MORE THAN A FEELING There's nothing wrong with feeling in your heart that Christianity is true or sensing that there is a God. I firmly believe that the Holy Spirit guides us and nudges us and that sometimes we are given divine inspiration that leads us to truth. However, I don't think that these feelings help anyone who didn't feel them personally. They're intended only for the recipient. Therefore, they don't make for a compelling case for Christ (to borrow a term from Mr. Strobel). Additionally, if we open up the floodgates of following our feelings, then we're not going to like where that leads. Muslims and Mormons have feelings too. Does that mean their religion is true? So, when it comes to being able to "give an answer to everyone who asks you to give the reason for the hope 23

ANSWER WITH TRUTH that you have" (1 Pt 3:15), we need to have something more than just a story about a feeling we had at camp one summer or when we've had a near-death experience and so on. After all, Mormons are notorious for claiming they have the truth because of a "burning in their bosom." And Muslims can have a feeling that they are right. And so can Hindus and atheists. So, let's not just join in the feelings party. Let's have good reasons for our faith. Let's have evidence. HOW TO SHARE THE GOSPEL A lot of Christians don't know what to do if the ball is lobbed slowly right down the middle of the plate. Tactic 6, Lead with the Bad News It's really difficult for people to grasp the good news (the gospel) until they first comprehend the bad news. "Jesus saves" or "Jesus is the answer" will fall on deaf ears in a culture as affluent as ours. "Jesus saves from what? Do I look like I need saving? Jesus is the answer? I didn't know there was a question. Have I found Jesus? I didn't know he was lost." 76

HOW TO SHARE THE GOSPEL There are certainly many great ways to share the gospel. I'm not saying this is the exclusive, correct way to do it, but this works for me. I have learned this from Greg Koukl. It's easy to use and nonconfrontational, and it appeals to our sense of justice and love. Here is a great way to start: Do you think people should be held accountable for their moral crimes? Yeah, me too. Have you ever committed any moral crimes? I know I have. I've stolen, lied, cheated, and hurt people. So, we both agree that moral crimes ought to be punished. And we both agree that we've committed moral crimes. I would call that … bad news. The apostle Paul wrote that no one is good enough to be in the presence of God. He says that God is so perfectly good that no sinner could even be near him. God is perfectly good, and we're all broken sinners. Imagine a judge who is famous for being perfectly just. No criminal goes unpunished in his court. And this same judge is also known for being perfectly loving. He is the perfect picture of grace and love. Now, imagine that this judge's son is arrested for stealing from 77

a store down the street. The son pleads guilty and stands before his father, the judge. Now, this judge has a dilemma. How can he remain perfectly just in the law and perfectly loving toward his child? So, here's what the judge did. He found the young man guilty and sentenced him to the maximum fine according to the law. Then he stood up, took off his robe, walked down from his bench, and paid the fine for his son. That is perfect justice and perfect love. And that is exactly what God did for us when we stood before him, guilty of sins. He found us guilty and sentenced us to death (the apostle Paul wrote to the Romans that the penalty for sin is death). Then God took off his divine glory, took on human form named Jesus, lived a perfect life, and died as the ultimate payment for our sin. All we must do is accept that payment. Say, "God, I am a sinner, and I thank you for your payment on the cross on my behalf." Can you imagine if that son stood there in the courtroom and said to the judge, "No thanks, I'll try to pay the fine myself," or "Maybe there are multiple paths to my redemption"? He'd be a fool. The payment is his life. And the God of the universe has already made that payment. 78
